She has taken to home life brilliantly and despite having never lived in a home before, she has been clean around the house, with no accidents indoors. She settles beautifully overnight and loves curling up somewhere warm and cosy with her foster family. In fact, she gets so comfortable at bedtime that convincing her to go outside for her final toilet trip can sometimes be a challenge!

Clippa absolutely LOVES her walks and thrives on getting out and exploring the world around her. She would love an active home that understands the busy, intelligent and inquisitive nature of spaniels and can provide her with plenty of mental and physical stimulation. She already walks well on lead most of the time, although she can sometimes be strong and excitable when out and about. At the moment, her recall is non-existent in secure fields as she is far too busy exploring to pay attention, so her adopters will need to be committed to continuing her training with this.

She could happily be homed as an only dog as long as her adopters were around most of the time and had plenty of time to spend with her, but she could also be homed with another kind and playful resident dog. She could live with gentle dog savvy teenagers but cannot live with cats or small furries.

Since arriving in foster, Clippa has shown signs of separation anxiety and struggles being away from her people even for short periods. Her fosterers are already working hard on building her confidence and independence, and her future adopters will need to continue this, gradually building up the time she is left.

Clippa arrived in our care with bilateral conjunctivitis which responded very well to treatment and continues to be monitored. She also had one tooth removed at the time of her spay.

Clippa is such a happy, loving and affectionate little girl who has embraced her new life with enthusiasm. Whether she is out exploring, making new friends, or snuggled up for cuddles, she throws herself wholeheartedly into everything she does. We think she will make an absolutely wonderful companion for a very lucky family.

Clippa has been given the Gift of Life by Carol Unsworth

Clippa has now moved into foster and is settling in brilliantly! On arrival, she made it her mission to explore every inch of the garden, so she will definitely need a very secure garden with a minimum of 6ft fencing as she is an adventurous and inquisitive girl.

She has settled beautifully into home life and gets on really well with the resident dogs, although her true preference is definitely humans! Every visitor is instantly greeted by Clippa demanding cuddles, belly rubs and attention. She is such a happy, affectionate little girl who absolutely adores people.

Clippa LOVES her walks and exploring the world around her. She would thrive in an active home that understands the busy, inquisitive nature of spaniels and can keep both her mind and body stimulated. She enjoys playing with polite dogs too, but people will always be her favourite.

At the moment, Clippa has zero recall in secure fields and is far too busy exploring to pay any attention to you! She will need lots of training and practice with this before potentially being allowed off lead and her adopters must be prepared to put the work in.

She has met older children calmly and could be homed with kind and gentle, dog savvy teenagers. She has taken everything in her stride. Around the home she is very clean, has never had a single accident indoors, and settles beautifully overnight sleeping upstairs in the bedroom. In fact, she gets so cosy at bedtime that getting her outside for her final toilet trip can be a challenge and her Foster Dad often has to carry her out because she doesn’t want to leave her warm snuggle spot!

Meal times can sometimes be entertaining as Clippa tends to take a bite, wander off to investigate something else, then come back for another bite as there is always something more exciting to check out! Her absolute favourite things in life are walkies, cheese, and snuggling up with Foster Daddy.

Since arriving in foster, Clippa has decided she loves people so much that she finds being away from them difficult, even for 30 seconds. Her fosterers are already working on her separation anxiety with her and gradually increasing the time she is left, but any future adopters will need to continue working with her and making this a priority with patience, consistency and understanding.

Clippa is an absolute angel girl. She is lovely and affectionate and will snuggle right in for cuddles. Clippa also loves going on long walks and meeting new people.

This week Clippa has been enjoying the sunshine at the rescue, and has been out on some super walks with our lovely volunteers and having plenty of sniff time in our play yards.

Clippa would love a home where she will be able to join in on all the family adventures and have plenty of cuddles.

Clippa is an absolutely lovely Springer Spaniel who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. She is a very affectionate girl who loves human company and will happily snuggle right into you for a cuddle.

Clippa gets on wonderfully with her kennel friends and enjoys their company, so would be happy to share her home with another kind resident dog. She is also confident enough that we would consider homing her as an only dog in the right circumstances, as long as her adopters were around most of the time. She couldn't be homed with cats or small furries. We would consider homing her with dog savvy teenagers.

As Clippa has never lived in a home before, she will need support with house training and learning the basics of home life. With her friendly and affectionate nature, she is likely to enjoy this learning process with her new family.

Clippa can already walk on a lead and enjoys getting out and about, though she can be a little strong at times and can pull, so she will benefit from ongoing guidance and gentle training to help her with her lead manners. She would love to find an active home where she can go on lots of walks and adventures!

Clippa arrived with bilateral conjunctivitis which is already looking much better following veterinary care and will continue to be monitored. She has had 1 tooth removed at the time of her spay.

Clippa is a loving, affectionate girl who clearly enjoys being close to people and other dogs and we think that she will make a wonderful companion!

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Toby
description:
I need an experienced, adult only home with no visiting children.

I would prefer to be an only dog and no cats please!

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I can walk well on a lead but can be reactive to bikes.

I was born with some deformed toes, which have been amputated.

Toby is a handsome Cocker Spaniel who has been through a lot in his young life and is now looking for a special someone to help him write his next chapter. Originally coming to us from a breeder at just three months old, Toby was a very scared little puppy who found the world overwhelming. Although he found a loving home, he was sadly returned to our care after his adopters felt unable to manage some of his behaviours.

Toby is still a sensitive soul and will need a calm, adult-only home with no visiting children. His new family should have experience with worried or complex dogs and be confident in helping him feel safe. He will need a home without cats as he has met them at the rescue, and was too interested. While he is generally affectionate and loving, Toby has shown some resource guarding tendencies, such as grumbling when people pass by his bed or the sofa when he is resting there. He did once nip his adopter. This was an isolated incident, but it’s important that any new adopters are comfortable managing and working with these behaviours using gentle, positive methods. That said, since being back with us Toby has come out of his shell beautifully. He greets staff with a wagging tail, and absolutely loves attention, affection, and food, especially food! Those who work with him absolutely adore him and think he is the most special and wonderful boy!

Toby will be looking for a home where he can be the only dog. He did live with another dog previously and while they did get along, he did sometimes guard his adopter’s attention, so for this reason he is best suited to being homed as an only dog.

Out and about, Toby enjoys his walks and is lovely on and off the lead. He has good recall and mixes well with other dogs, although he can be reactive to bicycles, so should only be let off lead in safe, secure areas away from the public.

Toby was born with some deformed toes that were later amputated. He has always had a slightly unusual gait due to this, but our vets have noted he does also have a luxating patella and is on pain relief for this at the moment, but this may improve with gentle exercise. He also has a naturally short tail, which just adds to his unique charm.

Toby is a sweet, loving boy who truly wants to connect. He has already won the hearts of everyone here, and we’re all rooting for him to find a calm, understanding home where he can feel safe, build trust, and thrive.

If you have the time, patience, and experience to offer Toby the gentle guidance he needs, he will reward you with loyalty, love, and plenty of cuddles.

Tobywill need more than one meet and greet with his prospective adopters to allow him time to build a bond and feel comfortable with them before being adopted.

This week, Toby’s kennel mum celebrated his 1 year anniversary by breaking him out of the rescue for a couple of hours of fun!!Toby travelled good as hold in the car, and happily climbed into the back of the car & had his seatbelt harness fitted with no issues at all.He travelled to Pupstars Paddock with his kennel mum & another staff member who was new to him. He was absolutely brilliant with that staff member who could only sing his praises!!Toby absolutely LOVED the secure field, his tail did not stop wagging, he followed his kennel mum around everywhere and was (very gently) taking treats of both staff members.Once Toby bonds with you, he is the most loyal boy in the world. His circle of trust is only getting bigger, with more and more staff members falling in love with him as time goes on. He may have a stricter criteria than most, but it just means he is just an incredibly special boy in need of a special home. He is such a darling and we wish more people would be able to see it!

Toby’s one year anniversary with us is slowly approaching, and it honestly breaks my heart every day knowing he’s still not curled up on a sofa in a home of his own.

Toby is literally such an amazing dog when he knows you, all he wants to do is spend time with you, whether that be sitting with you, or going out for a gentle stroll!

Recently, I’ve been working on Toby’s lead reactivity, and he’s been doing incredibly well. He’s making such good progress, and I’m so proud of how far he’s come!

I really hope this is the last update I have to write about toby, hopefully his next updates will be coming from his new pawrents, showing us him blossoming in a home and how truly special he really is!

I’ve had the absolute joy of working with Toby for a couple of months now, and he truly is one of my more sensitive, special boys!

Once Toby gets to know you, he turns into the happiest, most affectionate little soul you could ever meet! He absolutely loves zooming around our big play yard, or heading out for a calm, gentle stroll along the lanes taking everything in at his own pace, Toby can be reactive towards passing cars or other dogs, it is something we are currently working on with him!

With the weather getting nicer, I decided it was the perfect day for a little adventure down to the river! To get there, we walk through a huge open field, so I popped Toby on a long line to give him some extra freedom to explore.I don’t think I’ve ever seen a dog so happy he spent most of his time rolling around on his back in the grass with the biggest grin on his face!

When we reached the river, Toby was a little unsure at first. He sat at the edge, watching the water. But after a few minutes of building up his courage, he dipped his paws in and that was it, he was happily paddling back and forth, enjoying every second. It was such a special moment to watch.

I truly adore Toby with my whole heart, he’s one of those dogs who just stays with you, always on your mind.

He can still feel a bit unsure when meeting new people and might bark or back away at first, but give him a little time and patience, and he’ll become your most loyal, loving best friend.

I honestly can’t believe Toby is still waiting for his forever home. He is such a gentle, beautiful soul who deserves the absolute best life has to offer!

Toby is a sweet older gentleman who truly enjoys the quieter side of life. He loves nothing more than snuggling up for a nap and being close to his people.

Toby still has a playful spark and enjoys his daily walks as well as pottering around the yard. He would thrive in a calm, peaceful home and is best suited to a household with no children or cats, where he can relax and enjoy his golden years in comfort.

I love Toby. He is so loved by many of us here at the rescue. He's a confident boy once he knows you, but until he feels comfortable around you he can bark and sometimes be a bit standoffish.

Toby is a big cuddle bunny, he absolutely loves a fuss, and will happily accept butt scratches any time of the day.

He enjoys going out for walks but absolutely loves being off lead and running around the big play yard, when his butt wiggles with happiness and excitement it really doesn't stop, it's super adorable. He likes chasing a tennis ball but he won't bring it back, instead he rolls around with it usually in his mouth and will then lay with it and play with it, he's a comical boy.

Again, once he's comfortable with you he's very easy to handle, I can get a coat or a jumper on him with no problems, I can pick him up and I can brush him with no problems, he had a groom recently and he was no issue at all.

It's a shame he keeps getting overlooked, yes he might have some typical cocker issues such as guarding, but it hasn't been a huge issue since he's been here, I can go in and out of his kennel, pick things up etc and he's been fine. I've also been yarding him with other dogs to try and socialise him more, and although he can be selective of who he likes, once he's found a friend then he just wants to play. He is a beautiful boy inside and out, and in my opinion anyone would be lucky to have him as part of their family.

Also he is kennel clean so house training shouldn't take any time at all.

Please, don't let Toby spend another year in kennels, he truly deserves to find his forever home, as they all do. It's getting colder, it's been snowing, a nice warm, cosy house with a loving family is what he wants, I know his forever home is out there somewhere so please come and find him.

Toby is still doing incredibly well with us here and hasn’t shown any signs of guarding, while this has stopped with us here we still are unsure how he will react in a home environment.

It is an absolute pleasure working with Toby and he always brings a smile to your face throughout the day. Toby is a gentle, loving and happy boy and is desperate to have somebody he can give all his love to.

Toby has definitely come out of his shell since he's been here, he still barks when you first see him but it's a happy bark and his tail doesn't stop, before he would stay for scratches for a little bit, but know he doesn't want to leave you and wants all the attention. He is a very loving dog.

Toby originally came to us from a breeder when he was around 3 months old. He was a terrified puppy who was worried about everything and would even squeak in fear when being handled. He was quickly adopted and found a loving home, but he has sadly been returned to our care as he was displaying some behaviours that his adopters felt unable to cope with.

Toby was homed with another dog and while they got along well, he did show some signs of guarding his adopter from the other dog, for this reason we now think he would be most suited to being homed as an only dog.

Toby can also sometimes grumble if people walk past the sofa or his bed and he is on them, so he will be looking for an adult only home with no visiting children, with adopters who have experience with dogs with similar issues and will be able to work on this with him. He did nip his adopter once around 18 months ago, but that was the only time that resulted in more than a grumble.

Toby is still quite a scared boy in some aspects and certain things will make him extremely nervous, such as the hoover or sometimes even minor things like the sounds of a drawer closing, but with confident owners with experience of scared dogs we do believe he will progress further. He would be best suited to a calm and quiet home, where he can settle in at his own pace and follow a routine.

He enjoys going for walks and is very good both on and off lead. He has good recall and gets along well with other dogs outside of the home. He can be reactive to bikes, so his adopters would need to be mindful of this and keep him on lead in any areas where he may encounter bikes and only let him off lead in secure areas.

As he has lived in a home before, we believe he is house trained, but he may need some reminders.

Toby is a sweet boy at heart and in the right home and with the right adopters we think he will thrive. Since his arrival with us he has been on his best behaviour and already adores all of the staff that work with him and they adore him too. He absolutely loves his food and has a true zest for life which makes us smile each day, so we are so hopeful that his perfect home is out there and will find him soon.

Toby was born with some deformed toes, which have since been amputated but don't seem to bother him, though he does walk with a slightly abnormal gait. He also has a short tail.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

name: Reet
description:
I would like a home filled with love and kindness!

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I’d enjoy one or two quiet walks a day and plenty of cuddle time.

I am living in a foster home and doing very well with toilet training

I have been spayed and ready for my new home

Reet has come to us from a breeder to find her forever home. When she first arrived, she was a little worried and quite overwhelmed, but it was clear there was a sweet and curious little Beagle waiting to emerge beneath her uncertainty.

During her time at the rescue, Reet gradually began to grow in confidence and show more of her true personality. She proved to be a bright, inquisitive girl with a larger-than-life personality and a real enthusiasm for life. She enjoyed meeting other dogs, exploring new areas, and learning about the world around her. With encouragement (and the promise of a tasty treat!), she even tackled challenges she was initially unsure of. Her confidence on the lead also improved greatly, and she soon began to enjoy her walks and adventures around the rescue.

Reet has now settled beautifully into her foster home, where she has continued to flourish. She is now very comfortable with home life, and the transformation in her has been wonderful to see. Her whole body language has softened, and she is much more relaxed and confident in herself. She is happy to try new things that would have seemed far too daunting just a few weeks ago, even stretching out and relaxing on the sofa!

Scurrying past people is now a thing of the past. Reet happily approaches strangers and will gladly accept a treat from just about anyone she meets. She remains a busy, active little Beagle who loves investigating everything around her, and her love of food certainly hasn't changed! She has settled brilliantly into a home environment, her toilet training is going very well, and she enjoys getting out on slow, sniffy walks where she can take in all the wonderful scents. She also has excellent recall, which is a lovely bonus as she continues to build confidence.

Reet will be looking for a calm and understanding home with patient adopters who are prepared to continue supporting her as she discovers just how wonderful life can be. She will need another kind and confident resident dog in her new home to reassure her and continue showing her the ropes.

Helping a dog like Reet is what makes fostering ( and adopting!) so rewarding. She has already come such a long way and has embraced home life with enthusiasm, curiosity and growing confidence. If you're looking for a sweet, affectionate Beagle with a gentle nature and a love of life's simple pleasures, Reet could be the perfect addition to your family.

Reet has been given the Gift of Life by Patti Tucker

Reet is now very comfortable with home life. Her whole body language has changed; she is much softer and more relaxed. She is happy to try new things, which just a few weeks ago would have been too daunting (like spreading out on the sofa!).

Scurrying past people is a thing of the past. She is happy to approach strangers and will take a treat from absolutely anyone.

Reet loves slow, sniffy walks, and her recall is excellent!

Reet had a busy weekend! We went to our local beagle meet in an enclosed field. There were 15 beagles and their humans too, and Reet made me so proud!

She came up and said hello to everyone, took treats and even had some cuddles from humans. She explored the field, but kept an eye on me and came over to check in every now and then. Such a brave girl.

Then back home and look who's applying for a bbq supervisor position! My husband is delighted that Reet doesn't seem to have that fear of men that is so typical of many ex-breeders. I'm sure it gets her extra treats from him, clever girl!

She is getting more confident at home too and now lets me stroke her. She still has a way to go, but it's very early days, and I'm sure she will become a happy cheeky beagle very soon.

Reet is settling well into her foster home. She is a busy, inquisitive little dog who absolutely adores her food and will take treats from anyone! Toilet training is going very well, and she has been joining us for walks outside too!

In fact, the only stumbling block for Reet is human touch. It still scares her, and she will move away from our hands as quickly as possible. She has only been with us for a week though and I let her settle in with no pressure.

Next week we have some bonding activities planned, extra walks if the weather allows, some touch games - I will keep you posted on Reet's progress!

Helping a dog like Reet is what makes fostering - and adopting! - so rewarding. So if you like beagles, and have the patience, understanding, and commitment to help a sensitive dog learn how gentle human hands can be, then please apply for this wonderful sweet girl!

Reet has been my project dog for the past couple of weeks, during this time my main objective was to encourage this happy curious girl to blossom. She's still got a little way to go, but I'm so proud for how far she's come.

When I first started working with Reet her sweet, curious nature was already there, that's just who she is. Since then she's been meeting new dogs and people and building up the courage to explore new areas of her life at the rescue.

As you can see in the videos she's got a larger than life personality that seems to brighten my day. She's now walking perfectly on the lead with confidence in her stride. She's expressing herself openly around people and dogs in the yard. I also got her to build the courage to climb an obstacle she was wary of, with the help of food of course, she is a beagle after all.

Reet has continued her harness training today. She took some encouragement around a large group of people, but bravely continued despite her fears.She absolutely loves the freedom, and space of the big yard with her friends. And she certainty won't say no to a treat.

Reet has made wonderful progress this week. She is doing really well with her harness training and even ventured down to the big play yard for the very first time today, which she absolutely loved. She gets along great with the other dogs and happily shares her food, beds and toys. Her tail never seems to stop wagging!

Reet is a happy girl who has come to us from a breeder and would love to find an active home! She was a little wary at first, but she is really coming out of her shell as she continues to settle in and is showing herself to be a very curious girl. That being said, she is initially nervous when we approach so you do have to go slow with her and allow her time to warm up to you.Reet will be looking for a calm and quiet home, where there is another kind and confident resident dog in the home that she can look to as she continues to build her confidence. As she has never lived in a home before, she will need patient and understanding adopters while she learns about house training and walking on a lead.Reet isn't yet used to being handled and can be a little worried about this, but she is getting more used to this every day and with time and TLC, we think she will truly blossom.Once she has mastered her lead training, Reek would love plenty of adventures and will make a wonderful companion after she has had time to settle in and adjust to home life.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. name: Pandora
description:
I need a calm and quiet home & I can live with teens, aged 16+.

I will need a kind resident dog to take me under their wing. I have never met cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I’ve never been on walks before and will need a gentle introduction.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

18-05-26 Pandora has come to us from a breeder to find her forever home. She is a scared little girl who is worried to find herself here at the moment, but she is trying ever so hard to be brave. If approached slowly and gently, Pandora is happy to enjoy a little fuss and is beginning to show what a sweet girl she is.

Pandora is looking for a calm and quiet home and would benefit from adopters who have experience of ex-breeding dogs and understand that she will need plenty of time, patience and reassurance as she settles into her new life. She gains confidence from the company of other dogs and will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to be her friend and help show her the way. She can live with older, dog-savvy teens aged 16+ who are sensible and will allow her time to settle in at her own pace.

Having never lived in a home before, everything will be new to Pandora and her adopters will need to be kind and understanding as she learns all about home life. She will need help with house training and will also need to learn how to walk on a lead and harness, initially practising around the safety of the garden to help build her confidence. She will need an extra secure fence of at least 6ft to ensure her safety.

Although Pandora is not yet walking on a lead, we feel she will be best suited to an active home where, once she has gained confidence and learned all about the world around her, she will be able to enjoy lots of adventures with her family. With time, patience and the support of her doggy friend, we are sure Pandora will blossom into a wonderful companion.

Our vets have noted that she has a Grade 1 heart murmur, which doesn't require any medication but will need to be monitored by her adopters and their vet going forward. She also came to us with a fluid-filled swelling on the side of her face, which our vets diagnosed as a salivary mucocele. This occurs when a salivary gland becomes damaged, allowing saliva to leak into the surrounding tissue, where it collects and forms a soft, usually painless, pocket of fluid. Our vets have drained this and it has remained stable so far. However, it is likely to gradually refill over the coming weeks or months and would need to be drained again if this happens. The only permanent treatment is surgery to remove the affected salivary gland, which would need to be carried out by a specialist. Pandora's adopters would need to be aware that any vet treatment surrounding this would not be coverable by insurance as it's pre-existing.

Pandora has been given the Gift of Life by Lynne Hughes

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Trixie
description:
I'll need an adult only home with adopters with experience of ex-breeding dogs.

I am looking for a home where I can be the only dog & get all the love and attention.

I need an extra secure garden and home that can accommodate my needs.

I am still learning to walk on a lead, and will need my adopters to be extra secure.

I understand toilet training, but will need a reminder in a new home!

I have been spayed and am ready for my forever home! Read below for more info!

Trixie is a very sweet lady who has come to us from a breeder in search of a new start. Although her tail is constantly wagging, she will shyly approach for a gentle fuss and tends to stay away and watch from a distance until she feels safe. Once she builds trust, she is affectionate and will enjoy a snuggle and a gentle fuss.

In foster, she shown herself to be a nervous girl in new environments who still needed encouragement to engage with new people. She would often hang back and observe, but once more secure she would come forward for interaction and enjoy fuss and attention from her fosterer.

Despite the fact that she is still a nervous girl, she also isn't keen on sharing her home with other dogs and so we feel she would be happiest as an only dog in her new home. Although she has been in foster with other dogs, she can be jealous when her fosterer is showing attention to other dogs and has reacted towards them on occasion. She is fine with socialising with other calm dogs, but can be reactive towards barking or yappy dogs ordogs that are in her face too much or too boisterous.For this reason, we feel she would be best suited to an adult only home with adopters who have experience of nervous ex-breeding dogs. She has met cats fine, but due to her tendency to become jealous, we would not look to home her with cats.

In the garden, she has shown signs of digging and has previously tried to escape a foster home, so very secure boundaries are essential. Her new home will need to have 6ft minimum fencing height and her new adopters will need to keep her on lead in the garden until she gains confidence outside. She might enjoy having a sand pit to play in so she can enjoy digging in appropriate areas.

Trixie had been mostly clean in the house in her foster home, and seemed to be a star at toilet training. However, she may need some reminders in her new home as she settles in new surroundings.

She is beginning to walk on a harness but can still become startled and may try to back out if frightened. She needs experienced adopters, a secure garden (6ft+ fencing) as she has been digging in her foster garden, and additional safety measures indoors.

She will need a calm, secure, adult-only home and a gentle introduction to home life.

She has had a small lump removed from her left mammary gland and is recovering well.

Trixie has been given the Gift of Life by Tanya Player

If every day was a Trixie day, life would be lovely. Trixie is the sweetest and loveliest of dogs. Her favourite pastimes include tummy rubs and cuddles, and if there’s a home out there with a Trixie-sized spot on the sofa and a human to shower her with fusses, she would be in heaven.

When Trixie is happy, you know it, and she will bounce around you outside for a little play before parking herself next to you for more belly rubs. She loves sitting outside and just relaxing with you.

Trixie does also enjoy a walk (in very quiet areas); however, she can be very, very easily spooked and, if startled, will bolt and has done so previously. Any adopters would need to be very aware of this and work on building her confidence at home before considering outings. Adopters will also need to ensure a very secure setup for her at home, i.e. secure entrances and exits from the house.

Trixie prefers to have a human all to herself and, for this reason, she would like to be the only dog with a human who will be home most of the time.

Trixie truly is a gem of a girl with a heart of gold. A special and sweet lady, who longs for safe hands (and laps) to snuggle into. This super dog needs a little time and a lot of security,  give her that and you will have a bond for life.

Trixie is looking for a home that is quiet, gentle, patient and super secure on a practical level. Trixie is incredibly spooky and even if she trusts you, the slightest of frights in her environment and she will bolt. Because Trixie is a high flight risk she would be suited to a dog experienced home, ideally with knowledge of flighty and/or ex-breeding dogs.

It’s important that her new people have a secure plan for getting her out of the car on arrival home after adoption and are able to keep entrance and exits to the house escape safe. In return for your kindness Trixie will be the cutest of best friends. Trixie is looking to be the only dog and is suited to an adult home with no visiting children.

Trixie has been in foster with me for three weeks now and is settling into our routine fairly well.

When she first arrived, Trixie was very human-oriented and showed signs of jealousy towards other dogs, which led to a few altercations. She would often growl if another dog came close to me, as she wanted all the attention for herself. This is something we’ve been working on, but we still feel it is best if she is homed as an only dog in her new home.

From her interactions with my resident dogs, it’s clear that Trixie isn’t comfortable with small, yappy dogs. She does seem to prefer female human company, although she is continuing to build confidence more generally. She currently sleeps downstairs in an open, covered crate, which she uses as her safe space.

For the first couple of weeks, Trixie has slept downstairs quietly through the night with no accidents. Over the last few nights, she has been a little more unsettled and has had a couple of accidents, which seems to be linked to wanting more human attention.

Trixie is fed on dry Arden Grange and is currently fed separately, as she will try to eat other dogs’ food if given the chance. She was also caught eating poo, so I have started soaking her food in hot water and letting it cool, which she seems to prefer.

In her first week, Trixie did try digging in the garden and looking for ways to escape, but this behaviour has now settled. She clearly recognises me and gets very excited when she sees me, running over happily for fuss and attention.

She has been out on walks and does reasonably well when accompanied by another dog, but she is still quite flighty and easily startled. For now, she should only be walked in secure areas, as she will try to back out of her harness if she feels unsure and has demonstrated this once already.

Trixie can be complex at times, so she will need a calm, experienced adopter who understands nervous, ex-breeding dogs and can handle her with patience and care. It will be important to give her time to settle at home and build trust before introducing too many new experiences, especially.

Trixie absolutely craves human attention and is happiest curled up next to me on the sofa. As her confidence continues to grow and her jealousy is managed, she has all the makings of a very loyal and loving companion who simply wants to feel safe and adored.

Trixie is now in foster and is settling in well. She travelled well and was clean on arrival with no issues noted in the car or on entering the home.

Once in the foster home, Trixie has shown herself to be a very nervous little girl who is still unsure of new environments and needs encouragement to engage with people. She will often hang back and watch, but once she feels a bit more secure she will come forward and seek interaction. On these occasions she has chosen to snuggle close to me and will sit gazing and enjoying contact, although she does need to be gently encouraged each time as confidence doesn’t yet seem consistent.

It has quickly become apparent that Trixie is quite a complex girl in the home. She is very focused on attention and has already shown signs of jealousy when sharing human interaction. On one occasion, when another foster dog came onto the sofa, Trixie reacted strongly and became defensive. She can also be particular about other dogs in general and can be especially reactive to barking dogs, and has shown a clear dislike of a small dachshund in the home. For this reason, she will now be looking for a home as an only dog where she can get all of the attention for herself.

Outside of this, she is an active little girl and has already shown she is a determined digger, having previously managed to dig under fencing and patio areas. This will need careful management going forward with very secure boundaries required.

Overall, Trixie is settling in but is still very much finding her feet.

name: Nelly
description:
I will need an adult only home with breed-experience.

I’d like to be the only dog in the home and no cats please!

I will need an extra secure garden, at least 6ft+ where I will be safe!

I walk well on the lead but I am strong, so I will need continued training!

I have lived in a home, but may need a few reminders as I settle in.

I've started my vaccinations and will be spayed before adoption.

Nelly, also known as 'Nellyphant' amongst the kennel staff, is a beautiful girl with a huge personality and an even bigger heart. Nelly came into our care after experiencing a difficult past, having spent much of her life confined to a very small room and sadly being passed between several homes. Despite everything she has been through, her love for people has never faded. She absolutely adores human company and greets everyone she meets like they are her new best friend.

Nelly is a typical fun-loving Bulldog girl who is full of character. She loves attention, affection and being involved in everything her people are doing. She enjoys a good play session, especially when there is a ball involved! Tennis balls, footballs, honestly, anything she can throw around will make her happy. She is even learning that dropping the ball means the fun can start all over again. Once playtime is finished, she is just as happy settling down for a well-earned rest.

Sadly, the instability Nelly has experienced has left her feeling worried when she is left alone. She can become distressed when separated and will need a home where she is not left for long periods. Her adopters will need to build up time apart gradually, using patience and consistency to help her understand that her people will always return.

Nelly is currently learning about other dogs and is not yet comfortable around them. She is doing brilliantly with her muzzle training, which allows her to enjoy her walks safely while she learns that other dogs are not something to worry about. She can enjoy walking alongside calm, quiet dogs, but she may react if she feels overwhelmed, especially with larger dogs or if another dog barks at her first. Her new family will need to continue her muzzle training on walks and support her with positive, patient guidance.

Nelly can already walk on a lead, although she is a strong and enthusiastic girl who can pull when excited. She will need adopters who are confident handling her and who are happy to continue her lead training. She loves getting out for walks, having zoomies and exploring, but she is equally happy being a cuddle-loving couch potato afterwards.

Nelly has come from a home before, so she may already understand some household routines, but she may need gentle reminders as she settles into a new environment. She will need an adult-only home with breed-experienced adopters who understand her bouncy nature and can provide the boundaries, consistency and love she needs. She will need to be the only dog in the home, with no visiting dogs and no cats. She will also need an extra-secure garden with fencing of at least 6ft+.

Nelly is wonderful at the vets and loves the attention she receives there. She enjoys spending time with the staff, soaking up the fuss and showing everyone what a friendly and affectionate girl she is.

Nelly is a resilient, loving dog who has been through more than she should have, yet she continues to greet the world with excitement and affection. She is ready for a family who will spoil her, make her feel safe and show her just how wonderful life can be. For lovers of Bulldog breeds, Nelly’s silly personality and loving nature are sure to bring endless joy.

*Nelly has been measured by our local Dog Licensing Officer and confirmed that she is not considered a banned breed.

Nelly, AKA 'Nellyphant', plays football and she’s amazing at it! She also absolutely loves a herding ball. In fact, Nelly would play with any ball or toy for hours if she had her way, and we have to remind her to take breaks and relax for a bit.

Nelly has an exuberant nature, and no day is bad when Nelly's about. Nelly is now muzzle trained and proudly trots along the lanes near the rescue, donning her bright pink muzzle.

Nelly really is a delightful, confident, and human-loving dog, and as much as she likes to play, she will settle down and chill, often contentedly hanging out with the office staff at the rescue whilst they're busy working away.

Nelly's ideal home would have no other dogs and adult humans who have plenty of time to play and hang out with her, as well as knowledge and experience of bull breeds.

This super lady really does deserve to find a lovely home of her own soon.

Nelly, also known as Hippo amongst the kennel staff, is a beautiful girl with a huge personality and an even bigger heart. Nelly came into our care after experiencing a difficult past, having spent much of her life confined to a very small room and sadly being passed between several homes. Despite everything she has been through, her love for people has never faded. She absolutely adores human company and greets everyone she meets like they are her new best friend.

name: Holiday
description:
I need a quiet adult-only home where I can feel safe and loved.

I need a doggy friend in my new home. I haven’t met cats so I’m unsure.

I’m frightened and will need a secure garden to learn about being outside.

I am too scared to go out for walks right now and will need a careful introduction.

I am in a foster home now so am learning all about home life and house training.

I have been spayed and am ready for my forever home.

Holiday originally came to us from a breeder. She was adopted and found a lovely home, but sadly it wasn't the right match for her as the resident dog wouldn't accept her, so she came back into our care. Holiday has since moved to a foster home where she is settling beautifully and continuing to learn all about life in a home.

Holiday is a very sensitive girl who had very little socialisation before coming into our care. When she first returned to us, she found people incredibly worrying and was very hand-shy. Her first instinct was always to run away, and she wasn't comfortable being picked up or handled. If she felt pushed beyond her comfort zone, she could react by nipping out of fear, and she did nip her previous adopter once when she became overwhelmed during handling.

Holiday will still be looking for a calm, quiet, adult-only home with no visiting children. She will need adopters who understand the needs of scared ex-breeding dogs and who are happy to let her settle at her own pace, without pressure or expectations. She relies heavily on other dogs for comfort and reassurance and will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to help guide her.

Since moving into foster care, Holiday has made some really lovely progress and is growing in confidence every week. She now happily comes into the sitting room and chooses to settle nearby while her foster family relax. She enjoys being part of family life and likes to know where everyone is, often coming to find them and quietly checking in throughout the day.

She is now happily taking treats from our hands and has recently started giving gentle little nose bumps to our hands, which is a wonderful sign that her trust is growing. She also greets us with a wagging tail and will happily sit and listen while we talk to her, maintaining eye contact. When she first arrived, she would run away as soon as anyone spoke to her, so this has been a huge milestone.

Although Holiday is becoming much more confident, she still isn't ready to be handled much and is not yet comfortable wearing a harness or going for walks. Her adopter will need to be happy to continue building her confidence at her own pace, allowing her to decide when she is ready for each new step.

Minus her brief adoption, Holiday had never lived in a home before, but she has settled brilliantly into foster care. She is now 99% clean in the house and has established a lovely routine. She absolutely adores the other dogs, taking great comfort from them and often following their lead. She enjoys spending time in the garden and small secure field, where she loves exploring, relaxing and watching the world go by. She has also been left at home with the other dogs for a few hours and has coped very well.

Holiday is a very special little girl who has already come such a long way. She is learning that people can be kind and that home life isn't so scary after all. She still has a journey ahead of her, but with plenty of patience, understanding and the support of another dog, we hope she will continue to grow in confidence and enjoy everything her new life has to offer.

Holiday has been given the Gift of Life by Ellie Worron

Holiday has made some lovely progress since coming into foster care and is growing in confidence every week. She now happily comes into the sitting room and chooses to settle close by while we relax. She enjoys being part of family life and likes to know where we are, often coming to find us and checking in throughout the day.

She is happily taking treats from our hands and has recently started giving gentle little nose bumps to our hands, which is a wonderful sign that her trust is growing. She also wags her tail when she sees us and will happily listen while we talk to her, maintaining eye contact. When she first arrived, she would run away as soon as we spoke to her, so this is a huge milestone for her.

Holiday is now 99% clean in the house and has settled brilliantly into a routine. She absolutely adores the other dogs and takes great comfort from them, often following their lead. She loves spending time in the garden and our small field, where she enjoys exploring and relaxing.

At the moment, Holiday is not yet ready to wear a harness or go for walks, and she will need an adopter who is happy to continue building her confidence at her own pace. She is making steady progress and, with patience and kindness, continues to blossom.

Holiday is a very gentle, sweet little girl with the softest, kindest eyes. She has such a lovely nature, and it is a privilege to watch her confidence grow a little more each day.

Holiday has settled well into foster care. She is worried about being handled at the moment so we haven’t tried to stroke her or pick her up, we wanted to give her the chance to settle and feel safe first.

She will take treats from our hands, walk through doorways easily, even with us there, and she seeks us out if she doesn’t know where we are. This all indicates that it won’t be long until we can begin gentles strokes etc. When we are fussing our dogs she gets closer to watch and has touched my hand or sniffed my legs. Holiday is not as jumpy as I thought she would be, which is great, and we can walk quite close to her without her moving away.

When we are all settled in the evening, she comes into the sitting room to see where we all are and what we are doing. She isn’t phased by the TV and will often sit on a rug next to my chair.

We have a very small field which most new fosters are a bit scared of as they are not used to big spaces, Holiday loves spending time out there exploring and watching the other dogs play, we’ve even seen her do the odd little sprint!

She has been clean in the house and loves snuggling with the dogs at night in the kitchen, she is left overnight and we can go out for a couple of hours and she is fine as long as the dogs are with her too.

She has the potential to be the best dog and I don’t think it will be long before she is seeking attention and ready to try her harness again. She has very gentle eyes and you can see the sadness ebbing away a little more each day.

This week Holiday has been making lovely progress with lead work. We have been helping her get used to having a lead clipped onto her harness and trailing behind her, and she is becoming much more comfortable with this. Clipping the lead onto her harness is now far less of a concern for her, which is a big step forward, and when she is out in the yard she barely seems aware of it anymore.

She is also doing wonderfully with different people putting her harness on, which is a very significant achievement for Holiday given how sensitive she has been around handling. She is taking these steps in her stride and continues to make really encouraging progress.

Recently, I’ve been spending some dedicated time working with Holiday, and she’s making such lovely progress. She’s beginning to trust me more each day and has even allowed me to gently put a harness on her. Everything is done slowly and at her pace, with plenty of treats and reassurance, but this is a huge milestone for this sensitive girl.

We’ve also been working on gentle touch, always on her terms. When she chooses to come and sit beside me in her bed, we take things one small step at a time, building her confidence with each positive experience (and a few tasty rewards along the way!).

Holiday is starting to show more of her personality now. She’s often one of the first up for a treat and has even begun wagging her tail. It might sound like a small thing, but for Holiday, it’s truly something special.

She’s a wonderful dog who just needs a patient, understanding home where she can continue to grow in confidence and feel safe. Watching her come out of her shell is incredibly rewarding, and she’s so ready for someone to give her the love she deserves.

I am writing this update as someone who has not had much interaction with Holiday although I have worked with her on a few occasions. It is obvious from her previous update that there has been progress in her coming out of her shell and I was surprised to discover that Holiday would accept treats from my hand although when trying to smooth her coat, Holiday swiftly moved out of reach.

She is getting there but slowy and anyone wanting to adopt her will need to go slowly and earn that important trust. Holiday's regular carer can stroke Holiday when she is settled in a bed but only in one place. If a position is changed, Holiday will become scared and move.

It is apparent after speaking with Holiday's regular carer that for Holiday to make that important next step in her progress, it would be better for her to be in a home situation. Holiday is fantastic with other dogs and would definitely need to live with at least one other dog to give her that hope of progress and comfort. There is always someone out there for every dog and vice versa Holiday is waiting.

Holiday originally came to us from a breeder a few months ago. She was adopted and found a lovely home, but sadly it wasn't the right match for her and the resident dog wouldn't accept her, so she is now back in our care.

Holiday is a very scared girl who is very overwhelmed to find herself here and clearly hasn't had much socialisation before. She finds human interaction quite difficult at the moment and is very hand-shy, so she will need a patient and understanding home that can help her come out of her shell at her own pace. Holiday will run away from you when you enter her kennel and isn't keen on being picked up or handled. She finds this so scary, that if she feels like she is being pushed out of her comfort zone she can choose nip or bite, purely through fear. She did bite her previous adopter once when she was being handled as she became so worried.

For this reason, Holiday will be looking for a calm and quiet, adult only home where there are no visiting children. She will need adopters who hav experience with scared ex-breeding dogs and who will let her settle in slowly at her own pace with no pressure or expectations.

Holiday finds comfort and reassurance from the company of her canine friends and so she will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to take her under their wing. This dog will need to allow her to snuggle up to them and will need to be happy for her to follow them around. A kind resident dog will be an important source of reassurance and companionship for her as she adjusts to her new life.

Although she spent some time in foster and was adopted for a short period, she had never lived in a home before this, so Holiday will still need to learn all about home life and new skills like house training. She will also need to learn to walk on a lead and harness and will need to practice wearing these around the home and garden, once her confidence with handling has grown a little more. She will then need to practice walking around the garden until she is confident enough to venture further.

Since arriving back with us, Holiday has started to make some more progress and will now take a treat from our hands as long as she has a doggy friend next to her for support. With time, love and patience she will start to leave her past behind her and will truly start to blossom. Holiday's journey won't be a short one, but it will be so worth it when you see her tail wag for the first time or when she first willingly accepts some fuss and attention. In the right home, she will thrive, so please don't keep her waiting too long.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Gabriel
description:
I need an adult-only experienced home that understands me.

I can be an only dog or live with other dogs. I cannot live with cats.

I may be shy now, but once I have settled I would love a nice garden I can play in!

I love going for walks, and would love an active home!

I know some tricks & walk well on the lead but will need help with housetraining.

I have been neutered and am ready to find my forever home.

Gabriel originally came to us from a breeder, and for him the world felt like a very frightening place. He was worried of people, the new environments, and unfamiliar experiences left him feeling overwhelmed, and even something as simple as having a lead put on was a huge challenge. He needed time, patience, and gentle encouragement to begin to feel safe.

Gabriel was adopted for a short period, but unfortunately it was not the right fit for him, and he was returned after displaying unwanted behaviours in the home and unfortunately making the wrong choices when he felt uncomfortable in a situation, which did result in Gabriel biting. He will need a quiet adult-only home without resident or visiting children to ensure he can get the structure he is needing. Gabriel can struggle with visitors coming to the house so will need a home without too many visitors and the ability to put him in a different room when people do some over. He will need adopters who are willing to meet him multiple times to develop a bond with him!

Gabriel requires a highly structured, boundary-focused home with experienced owners who are confident, calm, and consistent. He is not a dog who should be allowed on the sofa or cuddling up with toys and we would not want him to be set up for failure. Instead, he needs clear rules, his own space, and guidance that helps him feel secure and understand what is expected of him. His adopters will need to understand that he has potential to guard high value items or sleeping spaces and will need to work with him. Respecting his boundaries is essential to his wellbeing and success.

He will need both physical and mental stimulation every single day, whatever the weather. Regular walks, enrichment, and brain games are a must to keep his active mind engaged and to help him remain balanced and settled.

Despite his difficult start, Gabriel has made remarkable progress. These days, he can’t wait for his walks and becomes visibly excited when he sees his lead come out. He now allows his harness to be put on and walks beautifully, thoroughly enjoying his outdoor adventures.

Gabriel is a very clever boy and a true foodie, which makes training both rewarding and enjoyable. He already knows cues such as “sit” and “paw” and loves learning new skills. He thrives with clear guidance, consistency, and ongoing training, and will do best with an experienced owner who enjoys working with dogs and building a strong, structured relationship.

Gabriel forms very strong bonds with his people and is deeply loyal once trust is established. Because of this, he has shown signs of separation anxiety and will need adopters who are home for the majority of the time. He is not suited to being left alone for long periods and will thrive best with someone who can make him a central part of their daily routine. While he is not a dog who seeks constant physical closeness, once a bond is formed he does enjoy affection and gentle fuss in his own way.

He could live as the only dog, enjoying one-to-one attention, or potentially alongside a calm, confident resident dog who respects his space. He will need a home with no cats or small furries.

Gabriel has worked incredibly hard to overcome his fears and has already come so far. He is now searching for a forever home that will offer him understanding, structure, commitment, and the chance to live a fulfilled life. For the right, experienced person, Gabriel will be a loyal and rewarding companion.

Gabrielwill need more than one meet and greet with his prospective adopters to allow him time to build a bond and feel comfortable with them before being adopted.

This week we’ve had a heatwave which has meant alot of our rescue dogs have needed their ears checked incase they’ve had any mucky build up - Gabriel was one of the unlucky few that have needed some TLC in this area!!We’ve recently had a new meds person who has been visiting Gabriel to do his ear cleans & he has been an absolute angel. He’s even been given the nickname Angel GabrielIt’s just a testament to Gabriel that yes he can be unsure of new people, but once he knows you he truly is such a wonderful boy. He just needs someone to take that chance on him, show him the love he’s desperate to have & he’ll return it tenfold.Gabriel has been waiting such a long time now, and seeing a boy so full of love & hope is heartbreaking when we can’t reassure him that his time will come soon!!!

Today, I worked with Gabriel again for the first time in months. I was a little worried he’d have forgotten, however, it’s safe to say the bond previously made still remains.Gabriel is such an excited little boy. He lives for treats. He’s such a clever and obedient boy when the treats come out. Usually, he’s full of energy and excitement. Once the treats are out, he’s still and sits like the perfect little boy we know he can be.As he can be very busy, I’ve always thought it essential to take him out on walks, the longer the better. As mentioned in a previous update, he loves walks! Once you pull the lead out, you get a similar reaction to the treats.The longer the walk the better. Walks are so good for this gorgeous boy. They keep him stimulated and help shave off the pounds he gains from everyone giving him treats!On the whole, I was so happy to see my boy again. So happy, so loyal, keen to go on walks and he listened so well. I’m so proud of the progress he’s made since I last saw him!Gabriel still can be a challenge and, he does not like everyone. Once you’ve made that bond with him though, it stays. Don’t overlook this boy. He’s such a beautiful, intelligent and fun dog!

This boy has my heart, once you form that special bond with him I do believe it's for life. He is very loving and enjoys a cuddle and a fuss from people he feels comfortable around, as his adopter you'll hopefully get that bond through spending time with him and letting him go at his own pace.Gabriel, as most bockers do, lives for his food and treats, whenever I run him in from being in the yard I always leave a small treat in his bed or somewhere in the back for him to find, and he absolutely loves this he gets so excited knowing he'll get a treat and runs straight to the back of his kennel to find it.He loves his walks and his time in the big yard. He's the curious, adventurous type who just wants to investigate everything, a 10 minute stroll up the road can take us an extra 20-25 minutes to stroll back because he likes to explore, but I don't mind, seeing him happy makes me happy I'd never want to stop him from exploring the world.Gabriel would need an experienced, adult only home (view profile for explanation), he would need someone who is active and can provide him with mental and physical stimulation daily. He is a very smart boy and can pick things up quite quickly which is why, with the help from some treats I think he would pick up toilet training rather quickly.Please don't overlook this handsome guy just because he's not your small and fluffy type dog, Gabriel is a special boy with a lot of love to give, and for that right person/people he would become a true companion and best friend.

Gabriel, a stunning Bocker with a huge personality and an even bigger heart. This handsome boy is full of life and enthusiasm, always ready to embrace whatever the day brings. Gabriel thrives on affection and absolutely loves a good fuss and cuddle — he’s a real people-dog who enjoys being close to those he trusts.

Gabriel is a clever lad with so much potential. He’s already showing us just how intelligent he is as he responds to basic commands! With the right guidance and training , we know he’ll flourish and continue to grow in confidence, not that he lacks any confidence! He also walks beautifully on the lead, making him a joy to take out on adventures.

Sadly, Gabriel has been returned to us, and we are now searching for the right forever home where he can truly settle and feel secure. Gabriel would be best suited as an only dog, in a calm and quiet home environment. We are looking for a home with minimal visitors and no children, as busy households can feel overwhelming for him.

Gabriel can display guarding behaviours, so he will need experienced owners who understand this and can manage the home environment appropriately to support him. With the right knowledge, structure, and patience, Gabriel will thrive and continue to build trust.

Gabriel has so much love to give to the right person. With understanding, consistency, and kindness, he will make an incredibly loyal and devoted time

Gabriel is a stunning Bocker who is full of life, love, and character! This gorgeous boy is bursting with energy and adores nothing more than a good fuss and a cwtch.He’s incredibly smart, and we just know it wouldn’t take long for him to be trained to a very high standard – he’s already showing us how bright he is every day. Gabriel also walks beautifully on the lead, making him a joy to take out and about.Now all he needs is a forever family who can give him the love, attention, and adventures he deserves. With his brains, energy, and big heart, Gabriel is sure to make someone the most wonderful companion.

Gabriel has come to us from a breeder and has made incredible progress since arriving with us! When he first came, he was nervous around new people and worried about having a lead put on, which made walks and new kennel staff introductions a real challenge. Now, he gets excited for walk time and happily allows us to put on both his lead and harness with no issues.This handsome boy is a true foodie and loves learning new tricks. He already knows “sit” and “paw,” so with trust and patience, teaching him more tricks should be a fun and rewarding experience. Gabriel needs a confident, experienced, owner who will provide clear boundaries and structure, as he can be cheeky if he thinks he’s in charge as well as an adult-only home where he can continue to learn. Gabriel has the confidence to be an only dog in a home with can allow him the dedication and time he needs, or he can be homed with a resident companion that he can continue to learn and grow from, but will need a home with no cats or small furries.Once Gabriel knows and trusts you, he is affectionate, loyal, and loves nothing more than sharing his goofy charm. Underneath his shy moments, he is a beautiful boy with a heart full of love. He is looking for someone who will continue his training, set clear rules, and enjoy all the affection, loyalty, and joy he has to give.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Toast
description:
I would like a quiet, adult-only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a resident dog to help me feel safe. I live with a cat in my foster home.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in (6ft+)

I'm harness training currently but need to build my confidence around lead walking.

I will need to learn about house training and lead walking

Toast is a delicate little girl who came to us from a breeder and has been slowly finding her confidence in the world. When she first arrived, she was very nervous and overwhelmed by everything around her, often running to hide in her bed.

Since arriving in foster, it has become clear that Toast is an extremely nervous girl and remains very unsure of new situations. She does not enjoy handling and can panic when she feels pressured, so she will need careful, gentle management and time to feel safe.

Toast is currently living with a cat in her foster home and can live with a dog-savvy cat who respects her space.

She will need a quiet, adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand nervous ex-breeding dogs and can support her without expectation or pressure. A calm, predictable environment will be essential for her ongoing confidence building.

She has come on in some areas, but handling still needs to be approached very carefully. While she has previously shown some progress with being picked up and having a harness fitted, this must always be done with patience and awareness of her limits, as she can become overwhelmed.

Toast is currently working on her harness training, and does allow her foster mummy to put one on her now, but she is not comfortable on a lead just yet and will need continued support and encouragement to build confidence. She is still learning about house training, and seems to be clean in the home when her foster mummy is around but can have a few accidents when she is left alone. With time and patience, she will begin to pick this up and understand the rules of house training.

She need a kind, confident resident dog in her new home to help guide her and provide reassurance as she adjusts to home life.

Toast arrived with a low-grade luxating patella on her left side, which may improve with gentle exercise. She has also been treated for mucky ears, right-sided otitis, hot spots, and dermatitis, all of which are improving with care. Her general health will need to continue to be monitored in her new home, with any future treatment provided as needed.

Behind her shy beginnings is a sensitive little dog who is still learning to trust the world around her. With patience, understanding, and the right experienced home, Toast will continue to take small steps forward at her own pace.

Toast has arrived in Foster and continues to be a very frightened little girl and can panic easily if anyone tries to pick her up, so she really doesn’t cope well with handling at this stage. Once she is settled in her bed, she is a little more comfortable and will allow gentle strokes and will accept quiet reassurance and talking.

She is extremely nervous overall and will need a quiet, adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand how to support a dog like her at her own pace, without pressure. She is also currently living with a cat in her foster home.

I can't believe toast has been here so long that she was due another haircut with me!She has come on tremendously for handling and as long as you're gentle she is content with being picked up, as well as having a harness put on. She is on the journey of learning to walk on a lead and it shouldn't be long before she achieves it.

I've worked with Toast for a while now and I have been able to see how much she's grown over time, especially when it comes to being groomed. The bath is still not her favourite place to be, but she tolerates it, and I think she quite enjoys the warm air of the dryer. She ends up with such a cute haircut because she is so well behaved when it comes to the styling.

This small dog has such a big and beautiful personality and I believe in the right home with another confidence dog she will flourish tremendously.

Toast was such a nervous girl when she came in and she has come on really far since then. I had the pleasure of giving her a much needed groom and although she was nervous she took to it like a champ. She will need grooming upkeep in her forever home to look after her evergrowing locks and I think with a few more pampers she will feel so much more comfortable with it.

She's currently having harness training so she can be more comfortable with the sensation and has gotten much better at being picked up and handled although she can still be a bit jumpy at times but once she's in your arms she's quite calm. She still has a ways to go but I'm so proud of her progress so far and can't wait for when she flourishes with her forever family.

Toast is a delicate little girl who has come to us from a breeder to start her journey towards a brighter future. Right now, the world feels like a very big and scary place to her, and her first instinct is to run and hide in her bed. However, once in your arms, she is easy to handle and accepts gentle reassurance with quiet trust.

Toast will need a calm and understanding home with patient adopters who can give her all the time she needs to find her feet. A kind and confident resident dog will be essential to help guide her, show her that the world can be safe, and give her the courage to explore new experiences.

As she has never lived inside a home before, everything will be new to her, from walking on a lead to learning about house training and family life. Gentle encouragement and plenty of love will help her begin to understand how wonderful it feels to be part of a home.

Toast arrived with a low-grade (Grade 1) luxating patella on the left side, which may improve with gentle exercise. She is also being treated for mucky ears and right-sided otitis as well as dermatitis.

Toast may be shy now, but behind those worried eyes is a sweet girl who just needs the right people to help her shine. With kindness, patience, and a little guidance, she will blossom into a loving and loyal companion.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Cassy
description:
I need a calm and quiet home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I don't mind cats!

I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

I’ve never been on walks before and will need a gentle introduction.

I am in a foster home now so am learning all about home life and house training.

Please read below for more information about my health!

Cassy is a sweet girl who has come to us from a breeder and is now ready to start her next chapter. She can be a little overwhelmed by new situations and may be unsure initially, but with time, love and patience she is already showing just how much progress she can make.

Cassy has now moved into a foster home where she is settling in well and learning all about home life for the first time ever. She is trying so hard to be brave, but doesn't actively seek attention yet. If she's on the sofa or in bed, she is happy to be fussed.

Cassy relies heavily on other dogs for comfort and reassurance, so she will need at least one kind and confident resident dog in her new home to help guide her and provide support as she continues to learn about life in a home environment. She has met cats and was very good with them, so could potentially live alongside a feline friend, with sensible introductions.

She had never lived in a home before moving to foster and is already doing well with learning new skills like house training, but does need more practice. Her adopters will need to be patient and understanding as she continues to build confidence and learn about the world around her.

Cassy has started learning about wearing a harness, but isn't feeling quite brave enough to go for walks yet, but this is something that her fosterer will work on with her.

This lovely girl is simply looking for someone willing to take a chance on her, offering the love, patience and reassurance she needs to truly thrive. In return, you'll have the privilege of watching her blossom into the happy, confident dog she is meant to be.

When Cassy arrived with us, it was noted that she had a cherry eye, which was repaired. This has recently popped back out, but at this moment is too small to repair again and so this is something her adopters and their vet will need to monitor. She has also been treated for conjunctivitis. She has had a dental with one tooth extracted, and our vets have noted that one other tooth has a slightly exposed root. This does not require removal at present but should be monitored.

Cassy has been given the Gift of Life by Dee Wickson-Dix

After returning to the rescue to have her cherry eye assessed, Cassy has now moved to foster once again. She is making good progress but is still very nervous. She doesn't actively seek attention yet, but she is trying so hard to be brave. If she's on the sofa or in bed, she is happy to be fussed.

She had her first walk on the lead in garden this morning, with the help of lots of primula cheese!! She will now move past me in a relaxed manner and enjoys cuddles with the other dogs.

She likes to be in the same room as me. She eats well and is food orientated and happily takes a treat from my hand.

She will need  an experienced, quiet home where she can progress at her own pace and be fully supported.

Her cherry eye has not caused any problems, and though it has recurred the vet felt like it did no require a second repair at this time, though will need to be monitored. I wipe her eyes daily.

Cassy recently arrived here in foster and is settling in well. Unfortunately her cherry eye has recurred so she will need to head back to the rescue centre to have this assessed, but she has really enjoyed her week in foster.

She is a lovely girl who is very food motivated and will happily approach with a waggy tail when treats are on offer. If food is around, she’s always hopeful for a little taste! She is very hand shy and can be skittish when approached, but with gentle reassurance and patience she will allow strokes and appears to enjoy them, even though she doesn’t actively seek fuss just yet. Sudden movements can startle her, so she benefits from a calm, slow approach. She likes to quietly follow me around the house but will move away if approached too directly.

She enjoys playing with toys and has a funny habit of carrying a shoe around in her mouth. Like many dogs, she may chew items left on the floor, although she doesn’t go looking for things to chew.

Cassy would suit a calm and quiet household where she can be given the time and space she needs to relax and settle in. She really wants to be brave, and with patience and kindness her confidence should continue to grow. She enjoys the company of other dogs and loves snuggling up with them on the sofa.

She currently sleeps in the kitchen with my dogs at night. She is house trained, and confidently uses the dog flap to go outside to toilet.

Cassy is a sweet girl who has come to us from a breeder and is still building her confidence. She can be a little unsure at first and finds reassurance in the company of other dogs.

She will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home who can help guide her and give her the confidence she needs as she settles in.

Cassy has never lived in a home before, so her adopters will need to be patient and understanding while she learns all about house training and how to walk on a lead. Starting with gentle garden exploration and quiet outdoor time will help her gradually get used to the world around her.

This lovely girl is simply looking for someone willing to take a chance on her, offering the love, patience, and reassurance she needs to truly thrive.
