Cassy arrived with cherry eye in her right eye that has since been surgically repaired at the time of her spay. She is also being treated for conjunctivitis. She has had a dental with one tooth extracted and our vets have noted that one other tooth has the root slightly exposed, but have advised that it is not removed at the moment and monitored going forward.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Nimbus
description:
I need a calm and quiet, adult only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

I can walk on a lead and harness and love to explore quiet areas.

I am in a foster home now so am learning all about home life and house training.

I am spayed and ready to find my forever home.

Nimbus is a beautiful girl who has come to us from a breeder to find her forever home. When she first arrived, she was very nervous and found everything quite overwhelming. Since then she has started to settle in and has come out of her shell more each day. Nimbus has now moved to a wonderful foster home where she is getting to experience home life for the first time.

While she is still nervous, she has settled into her foster home well. She really loves her food and will sleep all night quietly with the resident dog. She has been clean so far in the home, so is doing really well with her toilet training too. She has started learning all about walking on a lead and harness and now loves to explore quiet areas, so we think she will really enjoy going on walks with her new family.

Nimbus will be looking for a calm and quiet, adult only home with adopters who have plenty of time to spend with her and to help her settle in. She will need another kind and confident resident dog in her new home to be her friend and take her under their wing. It's clear how much she relies on her canine companions for reassurance.

It's clear that she hasn't been used to much socialisation or handling prior to arrival with us and she is still quite wary of people and isn't keen on being handled, but once in your arms she does begin to relax and settle.

As Nimbus had never lived in a home before moving to foster, her adopters will need to be kind and understanding as she continues to settle in and learn all about home life. With patience, kindness, and plenty of reassurance, she will slowly begin to flourish.

Nimbus has been given the Gift of Life by Scott Ferguson

Nimbus or Nell as she is known here, has been in foster with us for a week.  Nell is a sweet timid little girl and needs time and patience to help her settle into home life.

She has been clean in the house since she arrived and will now sleep all night with my dog. She loves her food, and is walking on a lead and harness in quiet areas. She really is enjoying her walks and loves meeting other dogs.

She is still wary of people and is not keen on being handled, but once you do pick her up she will settle.

She does rely on my dog and follows him everywhere, so she will need another dog or dogs in her new home, which would need to be a quiet, adult only home.

It will take Nell time to build her confidence, she has done really well so far so hopefully she will find her forever home.

With the recent heatwave, Nimbus has had a much needed and well overdue groom and I think we can all agree she looks absolutely beautiful!

She definitely appreciated being cooler in the hot weather, and it’s allowed her to enjoy the lovely weather the last couple of weeks (within reason of course!!)

Nimbus has been slowly finding her feet here at the rescue, and taking her time with opening up to us. She was incredibly frightened when she first arrived, but with time and patience from her kennel mums and dads, her true personality is slowly coming to the surface.

We find that a lot of our ex-breeders struggle more in the presence of men, but Nimbus is opening up to her kennel dad too. They’re not at the cuddling stage as of yet, but she happily comes up to him and gives him hand kisses so we think with routine, TLC and quality time spent together they’ll get there in no time!

Nimbus is a nervous, flighty little girl who has come to us from a breeder and finds the world quite overwhelming. She is touch-sensitive and clearly hasn’t been used to much handling, so she will need kind, patient adopters who can gently show her that human hands can be kind and reassuring. Despite her worries, once she is in your arms she does begin to relax and settle.

Nimbus relies heavily on the comfort and reassurance of her kennel friends, so she will need a kind and confident resident dog in her new home to help guide and support her as she learns about life in a home and continues to build her confidence. When she feels unsure, she will often retreat to her bed or a quiet corner where she feels safe.

As Nimbus has never lived in a home before, her adopters will need to be patient, gentle, and understanding while she learns all about home life and house training. Walking on a lead may feel overwhelming for her at first, so starting with gentle garden sniffs and allowing her to explore at her own pace will help her gain confidence.

Nimbus is looking for a calm and understanding home with adopters who understand the needs of ex-breeding dogs and are willing to give her the time and space she needs to come out of her shell. With patience, kindness, and plenty of reassurance, we are hopeful she will slowly begin to flourish.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Laurence
description:
I’d like a home filled with love and kindness!

I need a resident dog to help me settle in and I am fine with cats.

I'd like a secure garden with 6ft+ fences.

I can walk on a lead and harness and love to explore quiet areas.

I am in a foster home now so am learning all about home life and house training.

I have had 16 teeth extracted and would benefit from a soft food diet.

Laurence is a sweet boy who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a loving forever home. He has been described as an absolute gentleman, and it is easy to see why. Although he can be a little wary when meeting new people, with a gentle introduction he is quick to make friends and seems to enjoy some fuss and attention. He has recently moved to a wonderful foster home, where he is getting to experience home life for the very first time. He has already settled in very well and has even been house trained since the first day.

Laurence gets on wonderfully with other dogs and relies on their company for comfort, so will be looking for a home where there is a kind resident dog to be his friend. He is in foster alongside three resident dogs, two large dogs and one small dog, males and females and gets along with them all well. He is also living alongside a cat and chickens, so in the right circumstances he may be able to be homed with a dog savvy cat.

Laurence would love to find a patient, understanding family who will allow him to continue building his confidence at his own pace. He is a truly wonderful boy and we think he is going to absolutely thrive in his new home. Laurence can already walk well on a lead and harness and he is going to love going for walks with his new family. He will need a secure garden where any fences are 6ft+ to ensure he is kept safe.

Laurence has had 16 teeth extracted at the time of his neuter and would benefit from a soft food diet going forward. He has also had a cyst drained from his left hip, this may recur in the future but shouldn't cause him any issues.

Laurence has been given the Gift of Life by Deb Huggings

Laurence has now been in foster care for just over a week, and he has settled into home life remarkably well. He quickly adapted to the household routine and has been fully housetrained since his very first day.

He currently lives happily alongside three resident dogs - two large dogs and one small dog, both male and female - and has had no issues with any of them. Although he prefers to sleep downstairs on his own, you can often hear him listening to the gentle snuffles of the household upstairs during the night.

At home, Laurence is becoming increasingly comfortable with his foster carers. He now happily accepts gentle strokes much of the time and is beginning to enjoy human affection more and more as his confidence grows.

As with many newly rescued dogs, Laurence is still very aware of open gates and exits. For his safety, his foster home uses a second gate as an extra layer of security, and his future home will need to be mindful of this while he continues to settle.

Overall, Laurence is a wonderfully calm, gentle soul with so much love to give. He is looking for a patient, understanding family who will allow him to continue building his confidence at his own pace. In return, they will gain a loyal and affectionate companion who is already showing just how much he has to offer.

Laurence is a sweet boy who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a loving new home. He has been described as an absolute gentleman, and it is easy to see why. Although he can be a little wary when meeting new people, with a gentle and patient introduction he is quick to make friends. Once he feels comfortable, Laurence will quietly slink over for more fuss and attention, showing his sweet and affectionate nature.

Laurence gets on wonderfully with his kennel friends and relies on their company for comfort, so he will need a kind resident dog in his new home to help guide him and give him the reassurance he needs as he settles in and finds his paws.

As Laurence has never lived in a home before, he will need to learn all about house training, lead training, and the other basics of home life. His adopters will need to be patient and understanding as he adjusts to a completely new environment and learns that home life can be full of comfort, kindness, and love.

Laurence is a gentle soul who thrives on calm companionship and reassurance. With the support of a canine friend and a family willing to give him the time he needs, we are sure he will continue to grow in confidence and blossom into a wonderful companion.

A truly sweet and loving boy, Laurence has so much affection to give and will make a lovely addition to a patient and caring home.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Coco
description:
I could live with dog-savvy teenagers who respect my space.

I would like a kind resident dog in my new home. I am fine with cats.

I'd like a secure garden to potter around in.

I can walk on a lead and harness and love to explore quiet areas.

I am in a foster home now so am learning all about home life and house training.

I have had 2 teeth extracted at the time of my spay.

Coco is a wonderful girl who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. When she first arrived, she was quite shy and overwhelmed, but it didn't take long for her to come out of her shell and start to blossom! She is now a very friendly little girl who loves people and really enjoys having a gentle fuss, though she can still be skittish at times, but with love and TLC her confidence will continue to grow.

She has now moved to a wonderful foster home, where she is experiencing home life for the very first time. She is already settling in well and especially loves her food and treats!

Coco will be looking for a loving home where she can settle in at her own pace and where her adopters have plenty of time to spend with her. She has met children in her foster home and so could be homed with gentle, dog savvy teenagers. She loves other dogs and does rely on them for comfort, so will be looking for a home with another calm and friendly resident dog to be her friend. She is in foster with a cat and is fine with them so may be able to live with a dog savvy cat.

As Coco had never lived in a home before moving to foster, her adopters will need to be kind and understanding as she continues to settle in and learn all about home life. She is doing very well with her house training in foster, but may need some reminders in her new home. She has learned all about walking on a lead and harness and loves to explore quiet areas, so we think she will really enjoy going for walks with her new family.

When Coco arrived with us, we noticed she had a slightly unusual gait. To ensure there were no underlying issues, she underwent x-rays, which thankfully showed no abnormalities. She gets around very well and this doesn't seem to effect her, but she can't jump onto furniture so would benefit from having some doggy steps in her home to help her get on and off the sofa etc. She can also lose her balance if playful dogs bump into her, so for this reason she would prefer a home with a calm resident dog who wouldn't knock into her.

Coco is a sweet and affectionate little girl who has so much love to give. This girly pop will bring so much happiness and love into someone’s home. With time, patience, and the support of another dog, she will continue to blossom into a loving and loyal companion.

Coco has had 2 teeth extracted at the time of her spay.

Coco has settled in really well in foster and is enjoying her home comforts. She clearly loves people and approaches for hand licks and little strokes but can be a little skittish if you go to stroke her body. If approached slowly she will allow it and also allow you to pick her up where she snuggles in.

She loves food and will take treats and food gently. She has been completely clean day and night and asks to go out or uses the dog flap.

Coco isn’t interested in toys and prefers to spend her day either sleeping or having a little wander around the garden. She loves going for a walk in a quiet area away from the cars and will get scared if there’s lots of noise but deals with it really well and settles again quickly.

Coco's back legs don’t allow her to jump onto furniture so I feel adopters will need to have little steps for her to climb up easily. Although her legs don’t affect her generally and she walks around absolutely fine and can run around, she does fall over quite easily if another dog bumps into her. For this reason a calmer, older dog who also likes to potter around will be better for her.

Coco currently sleeps in the kitchen with my dogs at night and although for the first night she found it hard to settle, she now happily goes into the kitchen and doesn’t make a noise. Coco is a dream to foster!

Coco has met both cats and children and was absolutely fine with both.

Coco is the definition of loveable! She wants love and affection and is the friendliest little girl you will ever meet. But she doesn’t know how to receive it sometimes. She loves a head scratch but it takes time for her to allow you to touch her anywhere else without her running away.

Seeing her kennel mums in the morning is so exciting that she likes to hug their legs, her back legs were a concern when she first came in and have been checked, weeks later after lots of walk training and socialising and playing with her friends, you can barely notice that she had any issues with her back legs as she’s constantly playing or jumping up to say hello. Her unusual gait can be obvious, but it doesn't hinder her too much. This girly pop will add so much love to someone’s home

Coco is a wonderful girl who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. She is quite shy and overwhelmed at first, but once you spend some time with her she is a friendly girl who will approach us for little kisses. With time, love and TLC we think she will truly blossom.

Coco gets on wonderfully with her kennel friends and relies on their company for comfort, so she will need a kind and confident resident dog in her new home to help guide her and give her the reassurance she needs as she settles in and finds her paws.

As Coco has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training and the other basics of home life. With a gentle and consistent approach, she will continue to grow in confidence. She has started to learn all about walking on a lead and harness and with some more practice she will begin to gain confidence.

Coco could share her home with dog teenagers who can appreciate and understand that she will need a calm and gentle introduction to the home, along with her own space to settle in.

When Coco arrived with us, we noticed she had a slightly unusual gait. To ensure there were no underlying issues, she underwent X-rays, which thankfully showed no abnormalities.

Coco is a sweet and affectionate little girl who clearly enjoys human company. With time, patience, and the support of another dog, she will continue to blossom and become a loving and loyal companion.

10-06-26 Coco is a wonderful girl who has come into our care from a breeder in search of a new start. She is quite shy and overwhelmed at first, but once you spend some time with her she is a friendly girl who will approach us for little kisses. With time, love and TLC we think she will truly blossom.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Olive
description:
I'd like a calm and quiet, adult only home.

I could be the only dog or live with a male resident dog. I can't live with cats.

I would like a secure garden that I can explore.

I walk well on a lead and would enjoy one or two walks a day!

I have lived in a home so I know the basics.

Olive originally came to us from Romania as a puppy with her siblings and was quickly adopted. Sadly, she was recently returned as she was finding life in a busy city and busy household too overwhelming.

She will be looking for a calm and quiet, adult-only home where she can settle at her own pace. We feel she would really benefit from adopters with experience of Romanian dogs. Since being back with us, Olive has shown us just how sociable she is with other dogs and now happily shares her kennel with a lovely male dog called Cooper. The two get along brilliantly and spend much of their time together, often alongside Olive's best friend, Buck. We now feel Olive would happily live with another friendly male dog, although she can also be homed as the only dog. She does occasionally show some guarding behaviours around food with other dogs, but this is easily managed by feeding separately or giving her plenty of space at mealtimes. She cannot share her home with cats or small furries.

Olive can struggle with busy environments and traffic, so we feel she would be much happier in a quieter area where she can enjoy more rural walks. She can also bark at loud noises and strangers visiting the home, so this is something her adopters will need to be willing to work on with her. Her adopters will need to be patient, calm and consistent, helping her gradually build confidence and feel secure in her new environment.

Olive is house trained but may need gentle reminders as she adjusts to a new routine. She walks nicely on the lead and would love an active home where she can enjoy plenty of walks and adventures. She is a clever girl who already knows basic commands such as "sit" and "paw". Olive is a very sweet girl who enjoys being close to her people, happily sitting beside you, though she isn't overly fond of cuddles.

When Olive first arrived back with us, she was quite nervous and wouldn't approach us, but she has grown enormously in confidence during her time here. She now eagerly greets her kennel mummy every morning and has become an absolute joy to care for. She loves going out for walks with our volunteers, which has really helped her confidence continue to blossom, and she absolutely adores chasing balls around our big play yard. She is often at the centre of our larger play groups, encouraging all of the other dogs to run around and play with her. Watching Olive run freely and enjoy herself is truly heartwarming.

After spending the last seven weeks getting to know Olive, we can honestly say she has been an absolute angel. She has settled beautifully into kennel life, but it breaks our hearts knowing she has experienced the comforts of a home and is now waiting for another family to find her. She deserves so much more than kennel life and has so much love to give.

With time, love and TLC, we know Olive will blossom into the most wonderful companion. She really will make one very lucky family complete, so please don't leave this special girl waiting any longer.

Olive has tested negative for Brucella Canis.

Olive has now been with us for 7 weeks, and what an absolute angel she has been, it’s a pleasure being able to care for Olive everyday but it makes me so sad knowing that she has lived in a home and is now waiting in kennels for her forever home to find her!

Olive is always at the centre of the big play group, encouraging all the other dogs to run around and play with her, it is so heart warming watching Olive run freely!

Olive does now also share a kennel with a lovely male dog called Cooper, and the two get along brilliantly. They spend much of their time together, along with Olive's best friend, Buck.

From getting to know Olive during her time with us, we are now happy that she  could happily  with another male dog. With a few gentle introductions, she builds lovely relationships with male dogs and enjoys their company. She also gets on well with female dogs, but if there is already a resident dog in the home, we feel Olive would be happiest sharing her space with a male companion.

Olive can show a little guarding behaviours around food time when it comes to other dogs, but as long as she is fed at distance she is absolutely fine!

Olive deserves so much more than kennel life, she will really make your life’s complete!

Olive has now been here a whole month and has sadly had little to no interest!

I’ve been working with Olive since she first arrived, at the beginning, she was a very nervous girl who wouldn’t really approach me, but over time she has grown so much in confidence. Now, every morning when I arrive, she’s waiting at the front of her kennel to greet me.

Olive also now enjoys going for walks with volunteers it’s really helping her confidence build! She also absolutely loves chasing a ball down our big yard!

Olive does socialise really well with other dogs here at the rescue, however we feel she would be happiest as the only dog in the home where she can have all the attention she deserves, but she could share her home with a calm male dog.

Olive will make the most perfect companion for someone, please don’t leave her waiting much longer.

Olive originally came to us from Romania as a puppy with her siblings and was quickly adopted. Sadly, she has recently been returned as she was finding life in a busy city and busy household too overwhelming.

She will be looking for a calm and quiet, adult only home where she can settle at her own pace. We feel she would really benefit from adopters with experience of Romanian dogs. Olive would be happy to be homed as the only dog but she could also live with a male resident dog, although she can resource guard her food from other dogs so her adopters would need to ensure they were fed separately. She cannot share her home with cats or small furries.

Olive can struggle with busy environments and traffic and so we feel she would be much happier in a quieter area, where she can go on more rural walks. She can also bark at loud noises and strangers visiting the home, so this is something her adopters will need to be willing to work on with her. Her adopters will need to be patient, calm, and consistent, helping her gradually build confidence and feel secure in her new environment.

Olive is house trained but she may need gentle reminders as she adjusts to a new routine. She walks nicely on the lead and would love an active home where she can enjoy plenty of walks and adventures.

Olive is a clever girl who already knows basic commands such as “sit” and “paw”. She is a very sweet girl who enjoys being close to her people, happily sitting beside you, though she isn’t overly fond of cuddles.

With time, love and TLC, we think she will blossom into a wonderful companion, so please don't leave her waiting too long.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Nuzzle
description:
I would like a calm and quiet, adult only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I would like a home with a dog my size or larger. I'm fine with cats!

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I am in a foster home now so am learning all about home life and house training.

I have a Grade 1-2 heart murmur and have had 2 teeth extracted.

Nuzzle is a truly gentle, special little girl who has already come so far on her journey. She originally came to us from a breeder and, after a short adoption, sadly found her way back to the rescue as it was not the right fit for her. While in the home, she did make progress, but she was fearful of the man in the household and reacted out of fear on two occasions, showing that she needs very understanding, experienced adopters. She is now in a foster home where one of her fosterers is male and has settled in beautifully, which shows great progress for her.

When Nuzzle first arrived with us, she was an extremely nervous and flighty soul, spending most of her time hiding at the back of her kennel, too scared to join in or even accept touch. The world felt overwhelming, being in the yard, meeting people, or simply being handled was terrifying for her, and she lived in a constant state of panic and flight.

Since then, the transformation in Nuzzle has been truly heart‑warming. With patience, kindness, routine, and consistency, she has slowly begun to blossom. She now approaches her carers for gentle fusses and, when she feels safe, will even ask for more. She enjoys being groomed and is walking nicely on the lead.

She shared her kennel well with other dogs of a similar size and is now in a foster home where she is getting along well with the other dogs in the home. Nuzzle thrives when she has another kind, confident dog by her side and will need a resident dog, her size or larger, in her new home for emotional support and confidence-building. She can bond quite closely with her doggy friends and can get quite protective of them, so may prefer to be homed with just one other resident dog who can be her bestie. She is in foster with cats and is fine with them so could potentially live with a dog savvy cat.

Nuzzle is not a dog for busy or noisy environments and will need a calm, quiet, adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand ex-breeding dogs and are prepared to be patient, gentle, and consistent, allowing her to progress at her own pace.

Despite her fears, Nuzzle is a sweetheart who adores comfort, routine, and affection. She enjoys cosy beds, soft fuss, tasty treats, and the safety of familiar people and dogs. She is house trained and really enjoys the comfort of home life.

From a medical point of view, Nuzzle has a Grade 1–2 heart murmur which does not currently require treatment but should be monitored by a vet. She has had two teeth removed when she was spayed, and she also has a fatty lump on her side which currently requires no action.

Nuzzle has already achieved so much and continues to surprise everyone with her courage and gentle nature. She has incredible potential and, in the right home with the right support, she will continue to grow in confidence and trust. She is a quiet, affectionate companion who simply wants a safe, loving place where she can feel secure and cherished.

With the right family who will celebrate every small step and understand her past, Nuzzle will flourish beautifully and become a deeply rewarding companion.

Nuzzle recently went out with her foster parents to visit some friends and was such a good girl. She happily accepted treats from everyone, enjoyed playing in the garden, and had a lovely time with their little dog, Beau. She was a little nervous when their friends' sister and her husband arrived, but after a little time she found the confidence to let them give her a gentle fuss.

Nuzzle is such a lovely little girl who continues to make fantastic progress in foster care. She has become much more confident with her foster dad and now happily jumps up onto the sofa for cuddles with both him and his wife. She is house trained and is walking very nicely on the lead in quiet areas while she continues to build her confidence.

Today was my first grooming day with my foster brother. As my foster brother was due to be groomed my new foster parents thought they would treat me to one too and I was so brave and good. Although I wasn't very keen on the dryer and I'm still not very keen on being picked up, I did so well. Now everyone says I look so beautiful and fluffy!

Little Nuzzle has been with us for week now and it brings me to one question, and that question is why has this little darling not got a forever home already?She really is a little star from day one she has been nothing but a pleasure to foster. Her toilet training is 100% (only one accident on the first day she came), she gets on well with all the other dogs here and also gets on well with our cats. She's had 4 other dogs visit and was fine with them also.She sleeps well throughout the night, eats well, loves treats and will take them out of your hand. She walks on a lead with encouragement in quiet areas. She will allow you to fuss her but can be a little hand shy when putting on her harness and lead so does need vocal encouragement.Nuzzle is looking for preferably an adult only home with at least one other fur baby to help her continue with living her best life. Potential adopters must also be aware of her grooming needs.

Nuzzle is desperately missing her home comforts and I think she would love nothing more than to be back in a warm, cosy house. The weather at the rescue has been SO bad over the past couple of months and even though her kennel is set up like a palace because she’s so good, it’s still not the same as a home. She ADORES being snuggled up in bed and it’s definitely her happy place. I can only imagine how grateful she’ll be once she’s back on a sofa. We can dream!!!

This sweet girl completely has my heart and is breaking it at the same time. I cannot emphasise enough how easy she is to look after at the rescue. She quite literally doesn’t put a paw out of place in any aspect. She keeps her kennel and herself sparkling clean, she runs to and from the yard and back to her kennel perfectly every time, she shares everything including food and toys with her friends, and she’s easy to bath and groom. From the perspective of a kennel mum, she’s pretty perfect.

Her best friend got adopted a couple of weeks ago and she has been a little quieter since, but she now has a new kennel friend that she cuddles with a lot and has taken a shine to. I would so love for her to find her forever brother or sister soon because it would make her so happy to have a constant soulmate in her life.

Nuzzle has been back at the rescue for just over a month now and I have to say it’s been an absolute pleasure to look after her. She was on my section the first time she was here over a year ago and it’s been so lovely to see the difference in her. She was a very, very frightened girl back then and extremely panicky all the time. She was basically living in flight mode and it was so hard to have to watch. When she arrived back last month I was SO surprised when she approached me to ask for a little fuss on the first day. That is definitely not the same dog I knew!!

For the first couple of weeks I left her to decompress and settle back into my routine, and thankfully she did this pretty quickly. Since then, we’ve been doing lots of harness practice and she’s doing SO well. She’s been so much of a good influence that she’s even helped her best friend Denise learn how to walk too - so now they go out together on little strolls up the road and back. She’s still not perfect and won’t be up for going for big hikes anytime soon, but she’s getting better every day.

Her favourite thing in the world is being snuggled up in the back of her kennel under a heat lamp. She has a huge donut bed which she shares with Denise and you’ll find her tucked in for the majority of the day. They often enjoy a dental stick each while in the donut bed too, and it’s so sweet to watch!!

Nuzzle has SO much potential and I am so certain that in the right home with adopters who know what they’re doing with ex-breeders, she will thrive. She’s come such a long way and I know she has more to give, so please give her a chance soon.

Nuzzle is a truly gentle, special little girl who has already come so far on her journey. She originally came to us from a breeder and was adopted for a short period of time but has unfortunately found her way back to us as it was not the right fit for her. While in the home, Nuzzle did progress in some ways but was fearful of the man in the home and acted out of fear on two separate occasions without warning.

When she first arrived with us from a breeder, she was an extremely nervous and flighty soul, spending most of her time in the back of her kennel, too scared to join in or even accept touch. The world felt overwhelming, and every new experience, being in the yard, meeting people, or even being touched, was terrifying.

But since, returning, oh how far she has come! With patience, kindness, and the comfort, Nuzzle has slowly begun to blossom. She now approaches her kennel mummy for gentle fusses and, when she’s ready, will even ask for more. She’s learning to walk on a harness, enjoys being groomed, and moves confidently to and from the yard as part of her daily routine. She shares her kennel with a few other dogs of a similar size, and she gets along beautifully with them, sharing food, curling up together in bed, and finding reassurance in their company.

Nuzzle thrives when she has another kind, confident dog by her side, and in the right home, she will continue to grow in confidence and trust. She still relies on her canine friends for comfort, so she will need a resident dog of a similar size or larger. She also needs a calm, quiet, adult-only home with experienced adopters who understand ex-breeding dogs and are prepared to be patient, gentle, and consistent as she navigates life at her own pace.

Despite her challenges, Nuzzle is a sweetheart who adores comfort and love. She has shown that she can be fully housetrained in the home, but may need a few reminders in her new environment. She enjoys cosy beds, soft fuss, and tasty treats, and has a bright, promising future if given the right support. She does have a Grade 1–2 heart murmur, which does not currently need treatment but should be monitored by a vet, and she had two teeth removed when she was spayed. She also has a fatty lump on her side which requires no action currently.

Nuzzle has already achieved so much and continues to surprise us with her courage and gentle nature. She is a quiet, affectionate companion who just wants a safe place to feel loved and secure. With the right family who will celebrate every little step she takes, Nuzzle will flourish beautifully.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Nicole
description:
I need a calm and quiet, adult only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I will need a kind resident dog to take me under their wing. I have never met cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I’ve never been on walks before and will need a gentle introduction.

I am in a foster home now so am learning all about home life and house training.

Nicole has come to us from a breeder and is now searching for her forever home. When she first arrived, she was understandably feeling very scared and overwhelmed. It was clear she hadn’t had much handling or socialisation before, so human hands were quite worrying for her.

She has recently moved into a wonderful foster home where she is learning all about home life for the first time ever. She is settling in well and has a wonderfully inquisitive nature. She is still very worried about being handled and can bark when she's unsure, but she is making so much progress already. She will now take treats from her fosterer and absolutely loves her food.

Nicole will be looking for a calm and quiet adult only home where she can settle in at her own pace. She would really benefit from adopters who have experience with nervous ex-breeding dogs and who understand the patience, time and reassurance she will need as she learns to trust. She loves other dogs and takes great comfort from their presence, so Nicole will need another kind and confident resident dog in her new home to take her under their wing and help her feel safe and secure.

As Nicole had never lived in a home before moving to her foster home, she will need patient adopters who are ready to help her continue to learn everything about home life. She will need to learn new skills like house training and will also need to learn all about walking on a lead and harness.

With time, kindness and plenty of TLC, Nicole will begin to grow in confidence and let her personality shine.

Our vets have noted that Nicole has a slightly unusual heart rhythm, so she underwent a heart scan at our vets which showed some mild valve changes. At this stage she does not require any treatment and is expected to live a normal, happy life, although we would recommend routine heart checks and monitoring for any future symptoms.

Nicole has been given the Gift of Life by Shirley Bourne

Nicole has been in foster for a week and is a sweetie. She has a wonderfully inquisitive nature and loves investigating everything around her, which makes us chuckle! She is still very nervous about being handled and will bark when she's feeling unsure, but she is already making progress. She now takes treats from us and absolutely loves her food. She is also mostly clean overnight.

Nicole has come to us from a breeder and is now searching for her forever home. She is a lovely little girl who is understandably feeling very scared and overwhelmed to find herself here. It’s clear she hasn’t had much handling or socialisation before, so human hands are quite worrying for her at the moment, but we will continue gently helping her learn that hands can be kind.

Nicole will be looking for a calm and quiet adult only home where she can settle in at her own pace. She would really benefit from adopters who have experience with nervous ex-breeding dogs and who understand the patience, time and reassurance she will need as she learns to trust.

She loves other dogs and takes great comfort from their presence, so Nicole will need another kind and confident resident dog in her new home to take her under their wing and help her feel safe and secure.

As Nicole has never lived in a home before, she will need patient adopters who are ready to help her learn everything about home life from the very beginning. She will need to learn new skills like house training and will also need to learn all about walking on a lead and harness.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Tippins
description:
I can live with older children, aged 16+, who understand I need time to settle in.

I need a larger doggy friend in my new home. I haven’t met cats yet!

I would like a large, and secure garden that I can settle into.

I do walk on a lead, but am still gaining my confidence and will need support.

Please be patient with me, I haven’t lived in a home before.

I have been spayed and am ready for my forever home!

06-07-26 Tippins is a lovely lady who has come into our care from a breeder. She is a little apprehensive when she first meets new people, but she is already showing signs that her confidence is growing. Once she knows you, she enjoys a fuss and is happy to be handled. Tippins has a big personality and is a fun girl to spend time with.

At the moment, Tippins is feeling a little less brave and is finding comfort in the company of her kennel friends. We feel it would be best for her to be rehomed with at least one other confident resident dog for support and companionship. She has always lived with bigger dogs and seems happiest around them. Tippins can be a little bossy with less confident dogs, so she wouldn't be suitable to live with smaller or younger dogs. We will continue to assess her confidence while she is with us to see if she could adapt to being an only dog. She will need adopters with ex-breeding experience that can understand her needs.

As Tippins has never lived in a home before, she will need to learn all about house training and the other basics of home life. Her new family will need to be patient while she settles in and learns what home life is all about. She can live with older, dog-savvy teens, aged 16+ that can allow her time to settle into a calm environment.

Tippins already walks on a lead, but doesn't seem to be overly confident on this so she will need continued support as she learns, and once she has settled into her new home, she would enjoy one or two walks around the local area with her resident dog so that she can continue lead training.

Tippins is a lovely girl with lots of character. She's already showing us what a sweet dog she is, and we hope that, with time and patience, she'll continue to grow in confidence and enjoy everything home life has to offer.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Pip
description:
I’d like a home filled with love and kindness!

I would like a home with my daughter Patsy, but no other dogs please.

I'd like a secure garden to potter around in

I walk well on a lead and would enjoy one or two walks a day!

Please be patient with me, I haven’t lived in a home before.

I have a Grade 2 Patella Luxation and am missing several teeth.

PIP WOULD LIKE A HOME WITH HER DAUGHTERPATSY.

01-07-26 Pip is a sweet lady who has come to us from a breeder in search of a fresh start. She arrived alongside her daughter, Patsy,andthe pair are absolutely adorable and really rely on each other for comfort.

Pip is a gentle and caring little girl who has a very special bond with Patsy. The two are incredibly close and provide each other with comfort and reassurance, helping one another feel more confident as they experience new things. They are a wonderful pair who complement each other beautifully and will need to find a home together. They are the most wonderful pair and we will be looking for a home where they can remain together. They would need to be the only dogs in the home and wouldn't like to share their home with any other dogs.

Pip is a sweet and affectionate girl who enjoys the company of those around her and is a reassuring presence for Patsy. She helps her daughter feel safe, and it is lovely to see the bond they share. Their new family will need to appreciate that they are a pair and allow them the time and space they need to settle in together.

As Pip has come from a breeder, she will need time to adjust to home life and may need guidance with the basics such as house training, lead training and settling into a new routine. Her new family will need to be patient and understanding, allowing both Pip and Patsy to build their confidence at their own pace.

Pip and Patsy are a beautiful bonded pair who have already been through so much together. With a loving home, patience, and the security of having each other by their side, we are sure they will continue to blossom into wonderful companions.

Pip has arrived with a Grade 2 luxating patella, which may improve with gentle exercise, but will need to monitored and may not be covered by insurance. She has also arrived with 17 teeth missing and has had a further teeth cleaning and dental check up at the time of her spay.

name: Nana
description:
I need a calm and quiet, adult only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a male resident dog to help me settle in and I have never met cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I haven't tried walks just yet and will find this a bit overwhelming at the moment.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have some hormonal alopecia, but this should grow back. Read below for more!

10-05-26 Nana has come to us from a breeder and is now searching for her forever home. She is a sweet girl who is understandably feeling very scared and overwhelmed to find herself here. It’s clear she hasn’t had much handling or socialisation before, so human hands are quite worrying for her at the moment and she is very nervous of being handled. We will continue gently helping her learn that human hands can be kind. If pushed out of her comfort zone, she may panic and choose to make the wrong decision, so it's important her adopters take things slowly with her.

Nana will be looking for a calm and quiet adult only home where she can settle in at her own pace. She would really benefit from adopters who have experience with nervous ex-breeding dogs and who understand the patience, time and reassurance she will need as she learns to trust. She can be quite vocal when she first meets you, but she is quick to make friends when you offer her some tasty treats.

She relies on other dogs for comfort and reassurance, so Nana will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to take her under their wing and help her feel safe and secure. She gets along better with male dogs and so would need a male resident dog in her new home and cannot live with female dogs.

As Nana has never lived in a home before, she will need patient adopters who will be kind and understanding as she learns all about home life and new skills like house training. She will also need to learn all about walking on a lead and harness, first practicing in the garden as she builds confidence.

With time, kindness and plenty of TLC, Nana will begin to leave the past behind her and let her true personality shine through.

Nana has some hormonal alopecia, but this should start to grow back once she has been spayed. She has a small inguinal hernia that has been repaired and has had several teeth removed at the time of her spay.

Nana has been given the Gift of Life by Susan Stacey

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Lily May
description:
I need a calm and quiet home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I need a male resident dog in my new home and I am fine with cats.

I would like a secure garden to spend time in.

I walk well on a lead and would enjoy one or two walks a day!

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have been spayed and am being treated for a ear infection.

Lily May has come to us from a breeder and is now hoping to find her own special forever home. When she first arrived, she was a very worried little girl who was feeling quite overwhelmed by her new surroundings. Its clear she hasn’t had much socialisation before, so she will need lots of love and TLC as she settles into her new life.

Lily May did move briefly to a foster home, where she began to find her paws and settle into home life. Unfortunately she didn't get on with one of the female resident dogs in her foster home so she has had to come back to the rescue where she has settled back in well. She has since shown that she can be picky with which female dogs she likes to share her space with and so for this reason she will now be looking for a home with a male resident dog as she much prefers the company of male dogs. She was in foster with a cat and so could possibly be homed with a dog savvy cat.

Lily May will be best suited to a calm and quiet home with adopters who have experience with scared ex-breeding dogs and who will help her settle in at her own pace. We would consider homing her with dog savvy teenagers.

She is still very timid, but is taking everything gently in her stride. She can already walk on a lead, so she would enjoy one or two walks around the local area when she is settled in.

She meets other dogs well on walks and is even getting comfortable greeting their owners as well, showing just how brave she is!

As she has had limited experience in a home, Lily May will need patient and understanding adopters to gently introduce her to everyday routines. She will need support with learning about house training, however, she was already starting to do really well with this in her foster home.

She remains sensitive to everyday noises and movements, but is beginning to cope better with gentle reassurance and support. Lily May does tolerate being handled, and this will continue to improve as her trust develops.

Lily May is a sweet little girl who simply needs patience, love and reassurance. With the right family by her side, she will continue to grow in confidence and become a devoted and loving companion.

She has been treated for an ear infection, and has followed up with the vets that have said it has cleared up now.

Lily May is growing in confidence every day. She will happily come over to say hello and really enjoys a gentle fuss and being handled. She's doing well with her harness training and is making great progress on her walks.

Lily May loves spending time with her kennel mummies, and one of the sweetest parts of her day is greeting them with a happy, wagging tail whenever they come into her kennel. It's been wonderful to watch her confidence blossom, and she's becoming more affectionate every day.

I have seen Lily May make wonderful progress during her time with us. She is an incredibly sweet-natured dog who has formed a lovely bond with her kennel companion, Coan, and clearly enjoys his company.

Lily can be dominant around other dogs at times and would be best suited to a home with a compatible male dog. She walks beautifully on both a lead and harness, making her a pleasure to take out and about. She also loves attention, affection, and treats, which makes building a bond with her very rewarding.

Overall, Lily May is an absolute superstar. She is a lovely dog with so much to offer and can be handled when needed, continuing to show what a wonderful companion she will make in the right home.

Lily May has arrived in her foster home and is beginning to find her paws as she settles into her new surroundings. She is currently a very timid little girl, but is taking things gently in her stride. She has already accepted a harness well, and we will now slowly begin introducing lead walking at her own pace, which we hope will help build her confidence and support her ongoing progress with sensitivity.

Lily May would be best suited to a calm and quiet household where she can continue to build her confidence without pressure. She will need to live with another kind and confident, male dog, who will help reassure her and show her the way as she adjusts to home life.

She is still sensitive to everyday noises and movements, but is learning to cope with gentle support and reassurance. Lily May does tolerate being handled, and this will continue to improve as her trust grows.

She is also currently living happily with a cat in her foster home.

Lily May has come to us from a breeder and is now hoping to find her own special forever home. Lily May is a very worried girl who is feeling rather overwhelmed at the moment. She clearly hasn't had much socialisation before and so will need lots of love and TLC as she settles in to her new life.

Lily May will be looking for a calm and quiet home with patient adopters who have experience of ex-breeding dogs and understand that she will need time to settle at her own pace. She will need at least one other kind and confident resident dog in her new home to be her friend and take her under their wing, but this dog would need to be male as she doesn't want to share her space with a female dog.

As she has never lived in a home before, Lily May will need kind and understanding adopters who can gently introduce her to everyday life. She will need support with learning all about house training and will need to practice walking on a lead in the safety of the garden before she is ready to explore further afield.

Lily May is a sweet little girl who simply needs patience, love and reassurance. With the right family by her side, she will grow in confidence and become a devoted and loving companion.

She is currently being treated for a ear infection.

These wonderful souls may be older, have medical needs, or come with a few unique quirks—but they have endless love to give. Full of personality and deserving of a second chance, they come with a reduced adoption fee to help them find the perfect home. Could you be the one to give them the love they’ve been waiting for?

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Rachel
description:
I need a calm and quiet, adult only home where I can settle in at my own pace.

I will need a kind resident dog to take me under their wing. I have never met cats.

I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

i have never been on walks, but may be too scared and will need some time.

I have never lived in a home so I will need help to learn house training.

I have had a pea size cyst removed from my right shoulder at the time of spay.

Rachel is a very scared girl who has come to us from a breeder. She is understandably overwhelmed by her new surroundings and clearly hasn’t had much socialisation before. However, since being with us, she has slowly started to show small signs of progress as she begins to realise she is safe. Although she still finds being handled very frightening, we are beginning to see small but meaningful improvements in her trust, and she is looking for a home with adopters experienced with traumatised ex-breeding dogs who can provide the reassurance and stability she needs to continue building her confidence and learning about trust and kindness.

She will need an adult-only home that can offer a calm and predictable environment where she can take things at her own pace. Rachel will also need at least one kind and confident resident dog to help guide her and provide comfort, as she can still become easily overwhelmed, but she takes great reassurance from the presence of other dogs and shares everything beautifully with her kennel companions.

Rachel has never lived in a home before, so she will need very patient and understanding adopters while she learns about house training, walking on a lead and accepting gentle handling. The big wide world still feels quite scary for her, but she is beginning to show curiosity in safe environments, and gentle time in the garden will be a great way to help her slowly explore and build confidence. She follows a daily routine beautifully, happily running out to the exercise yards and returning to her kennel when it's time to come back in. She also tries her best to keep her kennel clean, and we feel she is likely to pick up house training very quickly once she has regular access to a garden.

Rachel is only acting out of fear, but we are continuing to see glimpses of a softer side as she begins to relax in familiar surroundings. Although she still tries her best to avoid being touched and may attempt to bite when she feels frightened, her reactions are already less severe than when she first arrived. Recently, Rachel needed to have her thick coat clipped during the warm weather, which unfortunately had to be done under sedation as she is not yet ready to be groomed. Despite how frightening this experience must have been for her, she coped far better than we ever expected, allowing herself to be carried to the vets and held for her injection once she was safely in our arms. We couldn't have been prouder of how brave she was. As her coat grows back, we will continue gently introducing her to grooming so she can slowly learn that being brushed doesn't have to be scary.

However, she still has the potential and will act on it to bite if she is backed into a corner, specifically when she is being picked up and handled. It is imperative her adopters understand these behaviours.

Rachel has so much potential and, with time, patience and kindness, we truly believe she will continue to make progress and learn that she is safe and loved. She really is an easy dog to care for in every other aspect – she is wonderful with other dogs, thrives on routine and never puts a paw wrong. She just needs a truly understanding home where she can continue building on the trust she has started to develop and finally blossom into the dog she is meant to be.

Rachel came to us with a pea-sized cyst on her shoulder, which was removed at the time of her spay.

Rachel has been given the Gift of Life by Tracy Davidson

Rachel’s progress continues slowly and steadily and even though she still doesn’t like to be touched, she is so much more content and calm in general. She’s now more than happy to hang out with me and lay down near me, and just this week she actually went to sleep next to me with her tummy exposed!!! I know that may not sound like a lot, but it’s HUGE for a dog like Rachel with such severe trust issues.

The past couple of weeks at the rescue have been extremely warm and Rachel started to struggle a little bit with having such thick fur, so I decided to get it shaved off. Rachel is still not ready to be groomed, so I did have to take her into the vets to be sedated. This is the first time I’ve had to actually pick her up since I started going the crate and touch work with her, but it had to be done. She does still try her very best to get away and she does still try to bite, but it is less severe than what it was when she first came in. Getting her into my arms was the most difficult part, but after I had hold of her I was able to walk over to the vets carrying her, and hold her while she had her injection without a fuss. I was so, so proud of her. She’s now a bit bald, but so much more comfortable and cooler. When her fur starts to grow back I am going to start introducing her to a brush so she can get used to it, as her coat does grow quite fast and it’s very dense which means it gets matted easily. It’s not ideal for a dog like Rachel needing to be groomed regularly, but I’ll try my very best to ease her into it.

Looking after Rachel on a daily basis is very easy. She follows routine beautifully - running to the play yards and back into her kennel when it’s time to come in. She tries her best to be clean with her toileting and I think if she had access to a garden regularly, she would pick it up really quickly. She also shares everything with her kennel friends perfectly and she never puts a paw wrong with other dogs. Aside from hating being touched, she really isn’t a problem at all. I really am hoping that she’ll start to get used to it soon, and I think she will … but being in a home would help massively. So I hope she doesn’t have to wait too much longer for that to happen.

For the past month, Rachel has officially been my “project dog”. This means I’ve been spending a lot more time working directly with her, and I set a goal for both of us when I started. Because Rachel’s biggest fear is human hands, that became my main focus — helping her feel more comfortable with being touched and gently stroked.

In the first week, I wanted to get her used to my touch without her panicking. When Rachel thinks she’s about to be handled, she’ll run around the kennel, jumping from bed to bed to avoid me, and will bite to protect herself if I get too close. I felt that all the running and panic was only reinforcing her fear, making things harder for both of us. So, I introduced a crate to safely contain her in a smaller space, allowing me to begin gentle handling in a more controlled way.

My hope was that she would start to realise that a soft fuss on her head or body isn’t actually that scary — that the anticipation was worse than the reality. Each day for that first week, I set up a crate at the back of her kennel and spent a short session gently touching her, just once a day to ease her into it.

On the first day, it took a little while for her to go into the crate, but she eventually went in with her friend. She was actually very good and allowed me to gently stroke her face for a few minutes, before becoming a little unsettled and starting to nibble my hand. By the second day, she went into the crate much more quickly and again allowed strokes around her head and under her ears. As I carefully moved further down her body, she became uncomfortable and nibbled a bit harder to let me know she’d had enough.

By the third day, she went straight into the crate, and I focused more on her body. She allowed longer strokes before becoming unsettled. The fourth day was very similar, but on the fifth day there was a real breakthrough — she let me stroke her tummy and chest without any nibbles at all.

After every session, I made sure to sit at the front of her kennel for a few minutes and give her treats so we always ended on a positive note (and to make sure she was still my friend!). Rachel is known to hold a grudge, so maintaining that trust was really important, and thankfully this work hasn’t damaged our relationship at all.

In week two, my goal was to remove the crate and see if she would choose to stay settled in a bed while I handled her. I wasn’t sure how it would go, but she absolutely amazed me. On the first day, she settled into a bed at the back of the kennel while I sat on the floor and gently shuffled towards her — and she stayed still, allowing me to stroke her. I kept things consistent over the next few days without pushing her too far.

By the fifth day, I introduced something new: scissors around her head. She needs a fringe trim, so I wanted to begin desensitising her to the sensation and presence of them. She did incredibly well — so well, in fact, that I managed to snip a tiny bit, which I hadn’t even planned to do. Because she was coping so calmly, I took the opportunity, and it paid off.

In week three, I continued trimming her fringe gradually, little by little. After a few days, I was able to clear the hair from around her eyes.

That’s where we are now. There’s still a long way to go, and Rachel will need plenty more time, patience and love, but I’m so pleased with how far she’s come. We’ve definitely made real progress, and I’m excited to keep building on it with her.

Rachel has been at the rescue for just over a month now and in that time she’s gained a lot of inner confidence, and has blossomed into a very cheeky and happy girl who always seems to be up to mischief!! She absolutely loves toys and gets very excited when I put them in her kennel every morning, and she also loves playing with them in the yards. She’s also very partial to starting ‘parties’ AKA getting all the dogs to have a massive game of chase with her, which she finds very amusing. Her favourite thing in the world though … food!!! She is one of the foodiest dogs I’ve ever met and is always at the front of the queue for breakfast, dinner, and treats.Even though Rachel has made progress in many areas, one thing she still simply can’t handle is being touched by us humans in any way. Her fear of our hands is so strong and she will try to bite to protect herself. This doesn’t mean she’s a nasty dog in any way, it’s just complete panic and fear. It’s something I’m trying to de-sensitise her to and I’m working with her as much as I can, but I do think it’s going to take a long time and a lot of consistency. We don’t know what she’s been through in her life that’s made her the way she is, so all we can do now is prove to her that humans can be kind, and hope that one day she’s able to put her past behind her.Rachel will need a very experienced home with people who will be extremely patient with her, understand her, and work with her through her worries and fears. She’ll need at least 1 resident dog to learn from, and absolutely no children. Things that are normal to most dogs such as going for a walk, being brushed/groomed, or even just having a little fuss, are alien to Rachel, so it’s important her adopters take all of this into consideration and be okay with it.

It may be a long and slow road to get Rachel to where she needs to be, but I promise it will be so worth it. There is no better feeling than a dog like her showing you love and trust for the first time!

Rachel is a very scared girl who has come to us from a breeder. She is a very scared girl at the moment who is quite overwhelmed to find herself here and clearly hasn't had much socialisation before. She is looking for a home with adopters with experience of scared ex-breeding dogs who can provide the reassurance and stability she needs to help build her confidence as she learns about safety, trust, and our intentions.

She will need an adult-only home that can offer a calm and predictable environment where she can take things at her own pace. Rachel will also need at least one kind and confident resident dog to help her settle in and take her under their wing, as she can be easily overwhelmed at the moment.

Rachel has never lived in a home before, so she will need patient and understanding adopters while she learns about house training and how to walk on a lead. Right now, the big wide world may feel too overwhelming for her, so gentle sniffs in the garden would be a good place to start.

Rachel is only acting out of fear, and we have not yet been able to see the girl she will become once she feels safe. She deserves so much love, she just isn’t sure how to accept it yet and will need a truly understanding home to help her blossom.

Rachel has come to us with a pea sized cyst on her shoulder that has been removed at the time of her spay.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

This dog can be homed with a resident dog.

name: Berry
description:
I need a quiet, adult only home that understands ex-breeders.

I need to be the only dog in the home, and cannot live with cats!

I will need a secure garden while I get used to my new home.

I walk well on a lead but I can be frightened, so I will need to take it slow.

I have lived in a home for a short period and may already know the bascis.

I have been spayed and am ready for my forever home!

02-07-26 Berry originally came to us from a breeder in 2022 and was adopted into a home, but she has unfortunately been returned as she was still struggling to live with the resident dog. While she socialises well with other dogs when out and about, she is looking for a home where she can be the only dog, as she has shown resource guarding behaviours around high-value items such as food and treats within the home.

When Berry arrived back here, she was a very worried little girl who preferred to keep her distance. Since then, Berry has made some lovely progress. Although she can still be a little nervous in new situations and with unfamiliar people, she has grown so much in confidence with the people she knows. She now actively seeks out fuss from her handler and, if they stop, she'll gently paw at them and even have a little cry to ask for more attention.

In her previous home, Berry displayed resource guarding behaviours around both dogs and people. Around people, this can be managed by giving her a little space and swapping the item for something of higher value, rather than trying to take it away. Her guarding comes from insecurity and likely reflects a life where she never truly had anything of her own. Because of this, Berry is looking for an adult-only home with adopters who understand resource guarding, have ex-breeder experience and are committed to using kind, positive management techniques.

We have been told that Berry is clean in the home, although she may need a few reminders while settling into a new routine. She walks nicely on the lead and doesn't pull, although she can be timid and easily startled, so she will benefit from gentle encouragement as she continues to gain confidence in the world around her.

Despite her worries, Berry has so much love to give. She is simply looking for a calm, patient home where she can finally feel safe, build trusting relationships, and enjoy life at her own pace. With understanding, consistency, and plenty of kindness, Berry will blossom into a wonderfully loyal and loving companion.

name: Dutch
description:
I would like a home filled with love and kindness!

I need to be the only dog in my new home. No cats please!

I walk well on a lead and would enjoy an active lifestyle!

Please be patient with me, I haven’t lived in a home before.

I am already vaccinated and neutered and ready for my new home!

Dutch is an incredible boy who has come into our care from a pound in Ireland in search of a fresh start. Unfortunately, we don't know much about his history, but what we do know is that he is one of the most loving and affectionate dogs you could ever hope to meet, with a heart as big as his personality. Dutch simply wants to be close to his people, whether that's following you around, leaning into you for fuss, or curling up beside you for cuddles. Being with the people he loves is all he really wants.

Dutch would love to find a home with adopters who are around for most of the day and can include him in everyday life. He thrives on human companionship and will make a wonderfully devoted shadow to his new family. He will need adopters who have experience with similar breeds and understand typical breed traits, including prey drive and the need for appropriate mental and physical enrichment.

Dutch would be best suited to life as an only dog. Although he can socialise well with other dogs of a similar size, he can become overexcited and mouthy, which may lead to frustration. Because of this, he is being trained to wear a muzzle during social interactions to help him continue building positive experiences. He cannot live with cats or small furries.

As Dutch has never lived in a home before, he will need support with house training and learning the general routines of family life. He is such a clever boy and always tries his very best to do the right thing, so we are confident he will enjoy learning with patient, positive guidance.

Dutch already walks well on a lead, although he can occasionally be reactive when passing other kennels. This is understandable in a busy rescue environment and may be something his adopters will need to continue working on. He is an energetic, happy-go-lucky boy who is more than ready to start exploring the world with his new family. An active home where he can enjoy plenty of walks, enrichment, training and quality time with his people would suit him perfectly.

Dutch could share his home with older, dog-savvy teenagers who can appreciate that he will need a gentle introduction to his new surroundings and his own space to settle in.

Dutch is bright, affectionate and full of love. He has so much to offer and is simply looking for someone to love as much as he loves them. For the right family, he will be an incredibly loyal, fun-loving and devoted companion.

Dutch has a passport and travel document.

Dutch has been given the Gift of Life by Louise Southward

Dutch, or as I like to call him, Dutchy, is honestly one of the most loving and affectionate boys you could ever hope to meet.

He's the kind of dog who just wants to be close to you. Whether it's following you around, leaning into you for fuss, or curling up for cuddles, being with his people is all he really wants. Watching him have to go back into his kennel after spending time together is heartbreaking because kennel life is just no place for a boy like him.

What makes it even sadder is that he hasn't received a single application. If I'm honest, my biggest worry is that he'll become one of our long-stay dogs, and my heart breaks at the thought of that because he simply doesn't deserve it.

He's such a clever boy and always tries so hard to do the right thing. He recently went on his first long walk with one of our wonderful regular volunteers and was an absolute angel. He took everything in his stride and made everyone so proud.

He's also been having play dates with our lovely girl, Swanny, to help him burn off some of that doggy energy while learning good doggy manners.

At the end of the day, Dutchy is just a baby. He's got the biggest heart, so much love to give, and he deserves a home where he'll be someone's whole world instead of waiting behind kennel doors. I know his family is out there somewhere; I just hope they find him before he spends another day wondering why no one has chosen him.

Dutch is an incredible boy who has come into our care from a pound in Ireland in search of a fresh start. Unfortunately, we don't know much about his history, but what we do know is that he is one of the most loving and affectionate dogs you could ever hope to meet.

He would love to find a loving home with adopters who are around most of the time and would have plenty of time to spend with him. He would need adopters who have experience with similar breeds and experience with his typical breed traits like prey drive etc.

Dutch would be best suited to being homed as an only dog as although he does socialise well with other dogs of his size, he can become over excited and mouthy which can lead to frustration. For this reason he is being trained to wear a muzzle when socialising. He cannot be homed with cats or small furries.

As Dutch has never lived in a home before, he will need to learn all about house training and the other basics of home life. With patience and guidance, we think he will adapt well and enjoy all the comforts a home has to offer.

Dutch can already walk well on a lead, though he can be a little reactive when passing other kennels, this is understandable in a busy rescue environment, but may be something his adopters will need to work with him on. He is an energetic and incredibly lovely bo who ie is happy-go-lucky in nature and is more than ready to start adventuring with his new adopters. He would love an active home where he can enjoy plenty of walks, enrichment, and quality time with his people.

Dutch could share his home with older, dog-savvy teenagers who can appreciate and understand that he will need a gentle and quiet introduction to the home, along with his own space to settle in.

He is a bright, affectionate, and fun-loving boy who is sure to make a wonderful and loyal companion for a lucky family.

Through the generosity of a Gift of Life sponsor, this dog was able to leave behind an uncertain future in Ireland and find safety and care at Many Tears. Every sponsorship helps us rescue and save more dogs in desperate need. If you would like to become part of a rescue story, please consider donating to our Gift of Life fund.

name: Swanny
description:
I could live with dog savvy teenagers who respect my space.

I can be an only dog or live with a larger male dog. I cannot live with cats.

I’m active and would love a large, secure garden to let off steam.

I have lots of energy and need an active home that loves adventures.

I do my best to keep my kennel clean, but may need help in a home!

Swanny is a truly lovely little lady who has come to us from a breeder and has been quietly stealing hearts ever since. With her unmistakable Beagle nose, Swanny loves to explore her surroundings and see what’s going on around her. While she can still have a slightly shy side, her confidence has blossomed beautifully during her time with us, and her sweet, gentle nature really shines through. She has become very people orientated and now absolutely loves some fuss and attention!

Over the past few weeks, Swanny has made wonderful progress. She is now walking beautifully on the lead and thoroughly enjoys heading out for walks or having a run around our big yard, where she shows her playful and inquisitive side. She also does her best to keep her kennel clean during the day, which suggests that house training in a home environment should be very straightforward with a little routine and patience.

Swanny has made friends with some larger male dogs here at the rescue and loves to go on play dates in our play yard with them. She can be quite picky with which male dogs she likes and can also be too enthusiastic and doesn't always read other dogs' signals, so she would only be suited to living with a calm, larger male dog who is very tolerant of her. Slow, sensible introductions would be essential. Thanks to her growing confidence, we now feel that Swanny could also live as an only dog in the right, understanding home where she can continue to thrive at her own pace or she can live with a larger, calm male dog. She cannot live with cats or small furries.

As Swanny has never lived in a home before, she will need kind and patient adopters who can help her learn the routines of family life. She could share her home with dog-savvy teenagers who can appreciate and understand that she will need a gentle, quiet introduction to the home and her own space to settle in. With gentle guidance, consistency, and love, she is sure to settle quickly and become a devoted and affectionate companion. An active family who enjoy getting out and about would be ideal, as Swanny loves having both her mind and body kept busy.
